title: Unify OpenAI Responses v8: seed routing + context-tag metadata passthrough
preview:
Summary: Add a compatibility layer for the incoming Responses v8 variant that preserves deterministic hints (seed/rng flags), forwards client metadata tags intact, and stabilizes streaming chunk boundaries for tool/function call reassembly. This PR centralizes the mapping logic so that other adapters (SDKs, private installer) can reuse the same translation rules. Motivation: Customers are adopting the new Responses v8 semantics (explicit seed + context-tag fields). Without a consistent mapping we see reproducibility regressions and toolcall reassembly bugs in streaming. This change reduces conversion drift and eliminates a class of flakiness observed in production canaries.

What this changes (high-level):
- Add responses-v8 adapter in pkg/openai/compat that maps v8 flags to internal RNG hints (seed-channel + deterministic-level).
- Preserve top-level client metadata (user_metadata, context_tags, annotations) through the bridge so downstream services (audit, metrics, autoroller) see the original attributes.
- Stabilize stream framing: introduce a framing anchor that pins toolcall boundary metadata on chunk edges to ensure assembled tool/function calls are not split incorrectly during resumptions or partial reconnects.
- Transparent fallbacks: if the client supplies both legacy "seed" and the new "determinism" object, prefer determinism but log the divergence for telemetry.
- Tests: unit, integration, and a new lightweight end-to-end canary to validate reassembly across connection restarts.

Design details: the compatibility layer lives under pkg/openai/compat/responses_v8_bridge.go and exposes a single function TranslateResponsesV8(reqBytes) -> InternalRequest. Key translation rules: map v8.seed -> rng_hint.seed (string), v8.determinism.level -> rng_hint.level (enum: none|soft|hard), context_tags -> request.metadata.context_tags (preserve order), user_metadata -> request.metadata.user_metadata, and structured_output.schema -> enforcement hints on the generator pipeline. For streaming, the assembler will now attach an "anchor-id" to the last chunk of a logical frame, and the reassembler reconciles chunks using anchor-id + sequence numbers to avoid mis-association when a client reconnects mid-stream.

Checklist: 
- [x] Add translation layer and unit tests
- [x] Wire metadata passthrough to audit and console plumbing
- [x] Streaming framing anchor + reassembly tests
- [x] Integration test against responses-v8 mock server
- [x] Benchmarked path for minimal overhead (added ~0.4% p99 latency in worst-case microbenchmark)
- [x] Documentation snippets added to docs/openai-compat.md

Commits (summary):
- a7b5c13: add responses v8 adapter and translation rules
- c3f8d21: implement streaming anchor + assembler changes
- e2d9a77: preserve and forward metadata fields to audit/console
- 9b1f4c0: add integration tests + mocked responses v8 server harness
- d4f2a55: docs and SDK compatibility notes; bump compat matrix

Files changed (high level):
- pkg/openai/compat/responses_v8_bridge.go
- pkg/openai/compat/translate_test.go
- pkg/streaming/assembler.go (stream anchor logic)
- api/handlers/responses_compat_handler.go (handler mapping + logging)
- internal/audit/forwarder.go (metadata passthrough hooks)
- docs/openai-compat.md (compatibility notes and migration guide)
- examples/compat/responses-v8-mock-server/* (test harness)
- sdk/typescript/compat/response_mappers.ts (minor mapping helper)

Testing and CI: Unit and integration suites were run in CI. First run showed one flaky e2e related to connection churn; I added a short jitter and retry in the mock harness and re-ran. CI checks: unit-tests (pass), integration-e2e (pass after re-run), lint (pass), benchmark-ci (pass but flagged small regression which is within acceptable SLOs).

Review notes and discussion (high-level):
- Diego: requested explicit handling when both legacy-seed and determinism object present; implemented and added telemetry for divergence (see commit c3f8d21).
- Priya: asked for clarifying docs on what fields are preserved and privacy implications; added section in docs/openai-compat.md and annotated the audit forwarder to redact PII by default.
- Marco: asked for perf numbers; added microbenchmarks in bench/compat_path and updated release notes.

Rollout plan: feature gated behind a runtime flag (features.openai_responses_v8_compat). Start with 5% of traffic in the Hosted API, observe telemetry (reassembly errors, deterministic mismatch rate), ramp to 100% over 72 hours if metrics stable. Dedicated and Private clusters get toggles in the console and an operator note for upgrade.

Post-merge actions:
- Add a short migration note for SDK consumers (TS/Python) explaining the new context_tags passthrough and recommended usage for reproducibility.
- Monitor canary for 72h and create follow-up if deterministic mismatch > 0.05% or reassembly failures > 1e-5 per request.

Related linear tickets: ENG-4532 (Responses v8 compat), ENG-4601 (stream reassembly fixes).

Notes on uniqueness: This PR emphasizes a single canonical mapping for new Responses v8 fields and a streaming anchor approach for reassembly; it is intentionally conservative about changing server-side generation semantics and focuses on compatibility and observability.

title: Structured retry metadata and scoped idempotency for streamed tool calls
preview:
Summary: Add a small, structured retry metadata header and an explicit idempotency-scoping mechanism to the OpenAI compatibility shim and streaming runtime so callers and downstream routers can make deterministic retry decisions for streamed tool/function calls. This clarifies Retry-After semantics and prevents idempotency key collisions across multiplexed streaming fragments.

Motivation: Customers observed inconsistent retry behavior for long-lived streamed tool calls (tool/function invocations) when proxies and CDNs normalized Retry-After or when clients used coarse idempotency keys across multiple logical tool invocations in the same session. The current behavior led to duplicate tool executions or silent rate limit loops for partial streams. This PR provides: 1) a compact structured header with deterministic retry window data, 2) an explicit idempotency_scope header to partition keys by logical call (stream, tool, function), and 3) mapping rules in the OpenAI shim to maintain backward compatibility with common OpenAI-style headers.

What this changes (high level):
- Adds X-Redwood-Retry-Meta: JSON-lite metadata for server hints (retry_window_ms, retry_type, token_cost_estimate) — consumers can use the numeric window instead of parsing Retry-After text.
- Supports Idempotency-Scope header to scope idempotency-key collisions (values: session, stream, invocation). When absent, behavior falls back to legacy single-key semantics.
- OpenAI shim maps OpenAI Retry-After to X-Redwood-Retry-Meta.retry_window_ms and emits structured error codes under error.type for consistent clients.
- Streaming runtime: when a stream contains multiple logical tool calls, runtime attaches scope-aware fragment IDs to idempotency resolution and stores KV-cache entries keyed by (idempotency_key, scope, fragment_index).
- Added server-side heuristics to avoid applying Retry-After from upstream proxies when noise is detected (e.g., 0/negative values, or >24h).

Files changed (high level):
- internal/openai_adapter/adapter.go: header parsing, mapping, and unit tests
- internal/openai_adapter/retry.go: structured retry metadata creation and mapping rules
- pkg/runtime/streaming/protocol.go: fragment metadata and idempotency scoping hooks
- pkg/api/headers.go: new canonical header constants and parsing helpers
- pkg/api/errors.go: new error.type values and mapping test fixtures
- sdk/python/redwood/openai_compat.py: compatibility shim emits new headers and preserves old ones
- test/integration/retry_metadata_test.go: integration scenarios with proxies and CDNs
- test/unit/idempotency_scope_test.go: unit tests for scoping logic
- docs/rate_limits.md: migration notes and examples
- charts/redwood/templates/config.yaml: opt-in feature flag for the structured headers

Commit summary: [3 commits]
- c1a2b3: add header constants, parsing helpers, and basic tests
- d4e5f6: implement structured retry metadata and OpenAI shim mapping
- f7g8h9: wire idempotency scope into streaming runtime and add integration tests

Compatibility and rollout:
- This change is behind an opt-in feature flag (api.structured_retry_headers). Default for hosted customers is disabled for 2 weeks while we monitor.
- The OpenAI shim continues to emit Retry-After and X-RateLimit-* headers for backwards compatibility. New consumers should prefer X-Redwood-Retry-Meta.
- Idempotency-Scope is additive; absence preserves legacy behavior. Customers using idempotency_key without scope will see no change unless they opt into scope enforcement.

Testing and QA:
- Unit tests cover header parsing, malformed metadata, and idempotency scoping logic (new test/unit files).
- Integration tests simulate a proxy that rewrites Retry-After and assert the runtime falls back to server-provided structured window when available.
- Performed manual canary on dedicated infra with sample workloads (chat-to-tool ping-pong) for 48 hours; no regression in p50 latency observed.

Rollout plan:
1) Deploy feature flag off to hosted for 48h while collecting metrics (error spikes, duplicate tool invocations).
2) Enable flag for 10% of traffic and monitor.
3) Expand to 100% after 7 days if metrics stable.

Migration notes for integrators:
- If you currently parse Retry-After text, migrate to parsing X-Redwood-Retry-Meta.retry_window_ms when available. Example header: X-Redwood-Retry-Meta: retry_window_ms=120000;retry_type=global;cost=15
- To partition idempotency keys across logical invocations, set Idempotency-Scope: invocation when issuing a stream-based tool call; server will use (idempotency_key, "invocation") as the backing key.

Security and privacy: No sensitive data exposed in headers. Structured retry metadata is intentionally small. We added validation to reject overly large header values to prevent amplification vectors.

Rollout observability: Added dashboard cards for structured-retry-metadata presence, idempotency-scope distribution, duplicate-tool-execution rate, and new error.type counts.

Release notes (user-facing): See release_notes field.

Example: header emitted by server: X-Redwood-Retry-Meta: retry_window_ms=60000;retry_type=soft;cost=3
OpenAI-compat example: server emits both Retry-After: 60 and X-Redwood-Retry-Meta: retry_window_ms=60000;retry_type=soft

Why this is different from previous work: Prior PRs standardized backoff envelopes and Retry-After text mapping. This PR focuses on a compact machine-friendly hint and explicit idempotency scoping for streamed/fragmented tool calls to eliminate collision classes that caused duplicate executions.

Notes: Tests initially flaked in integration CI due to recorded-proxy timestamps; fixed by tolerating small clock skew in test harness.

Acknowledgements: Diego (design review), Priya (runtime review), SRE on-call for integration infra help.

title: Self-healing traffic gate with prognostic rollback scheduler
preview: Motivation: We observed incident patterns where a small regression in an A/B arm degraded user experience slowly and only surfaced after sustained traffic. This PR adds a prognostic rollback scheduler and a self-healing traffic gate that combines short-horizon telemetry forecasting, a conservative anomaly scorer, and lightweight replay/bisect workflows so the system can proactively hold or revert risky promotes before widespread impact.  Summary of changes: implemented a prognostic score pipeline that consumes recent SLO/time-series windows and outputs a short-term risk forecast; added TrafficGate controller that enforces holdpoints when forecasted risk crosses policy thresholds; built a rescue-oracle that can run fast replay of recent 1% samples in a sandbox to validate rollback decisions; added operator-visible "rescue" and "replay" actions to Redwood Console so on-call can review diagnostics and trigger manual rescue if needed; introduced feature-flagging and gradual enablement config under rollout.policy.prognostic.enabled=false by default.  Checklist: [x] design doc linked (Linear ENG-4421) [x] unit + integration tests for forecast scorer [x] smoke tests for traffic gate hold/open actions [x] e2e replay against local-capture harness [x] console UI surfaced and smoke validated  Implementation notes: forecasting uses a lightweight ARIMA-like exponential smoother tuned for short windows to avoid model drift; anomaly scorer penalizes latency regressions higher than minor fidelity deltas; the rescue-oracle runs isolated synchronous replay (max 30s) and returns a scalar pass/fail + diff snapshot used to justify automated rollback.  Rollout plan: behind feature flag in main, enable in staging for 48h with canary percent=2% then roll to 10% with manual checks; enable prognostic auto-hold only after 7d stable metrics.  Related: supports both canary and A/B lanes and hooks into existing rollout audit logs and snapshotting.  Why different: unlike prior reactive rollback controllers, this one uses short-term prognostic signals and a bounded replay verification step to reduce false-positive auto-rollbacks.  Links: Linear ENG-4421 (design), SUP-1723 (support ticket that motivated fast replay).

title: per-request graceful-degradation: kernel compatibility shim + precision-hint API
preview:
Motivation: We continue to see tail latency regressions in mixed-precision kernels when models with large kv-cache footprints are routed through the 'fast' execution path. This PR introduces a lightweight compatibility shim and a per-request 'precision hint' header that lets the router/scheduler choose a conservative execution mode for requests that prioritize correctness over absolute token throughput.

What this PR does (single-string description): Adds a kernel-compat shim layer in the runtime that detects binary incompatibility or quantization sensitivity for a model+request combination and exposes a low-overhead boolean that the scheduler uses to avoid aggressive fusion/quantization fast-paths; implements an opt-in HTTP header (X-Redwood-Precision-Hint: prefer-correct | prefer-fast | neutral) and SDK flags that map to the same hint; extends continuous-batching logic to respect per-request hints by routing hinted requests to a separate lightweight queue that favors conservative kernels and smaller batch coalescing; introduces a KV-cache safety check that prevents prefix-optimized eviction heuristics from running for hinted requests; updates the metrics to surface how many requests used the compatibility shim and the delta in p50/p95 latency and token cost; includes tests and performance microbenchmarks.

Design notes and trade-offs: The shim is intentionally minimal: it does not change model weights or quantize on the fly. Instead it flips kernel-selection heuristics (no aggressive fused attention kernels, prefer fp16-safe paths, disable some prefetch transforms) and reduces batch coalescing windows to reduce peak memory pressure on hinted requests. This increases per-token cost but avoids correctness edge-cases introduced by certain fused kernels (observed in edge-case tests with malformed KV cache shapes). We chose a header + SDK-first API because it is the least invasive while enabling customers and platform teams to opt-in per-call.

Migration and rollout: The feature is behind a server-side flag (runtime.enable_precision_hint_shim). Default is off for all clusters; enabled in staging during canary; default for some internal teams with model reg tests. Rollout plan attached in the checklist.

Checklist: - [x] shim implementation and unit tests - [x] SDK header plumbing (python + ts) - [x] scheduler queue change and e2e tests - [x] add metrics and dashboard panels - [x] perf microbenchmarks (attached) - [x] rollout gating flag and docs

Related: Linear: ENG-4280, ENG-4297. Incident: SUP-1223 (customer reported hallucination/regression after aggressive kernel upgrade). More context: see internal doc https://linear.app/redwood/issue/ENG-4280/precision-hint-design (internal link).

Notes for reviewers: focus on runtime/shim correctness (no changed behavior for default-off), scheduler behavior (deadlocks or starvation between two queues), and SDK ergonomics. The PR intentionally avoids changing model catalog metadata until we validate metrics from canary.

Release notes: Adds an opt-in per-request precision hint header and a runtime compatibility shim to favor correctness over fused-kernel throughput.

title: Probabilistic length cohorter with pad-aware scheduling and KV-prefill heuristics
preview:
Motivation: long-tail sequence length variance continues to drive excessive padding and suboptimal kernel utilization in our continuous batching pipeline. Simple bucketing reduces padding but creates brittle boundaries and poor utilization when per-slot latency SLOs are tight. This PR introduces a probabilistic length cohorter that (1) forms soft cohorts by sampling length-affinity probabilities instead of hard buckets, (2) attaches a pad-cost estimate to each pending request using a small learned linear cost model, and (3) integrates KV-prefill priority to bias cohorts toward requests that benefit from warm KV caches. The goal is to reduce aggregate padding waste while keeping P95 latency within SLO and increasing kernel lane alignment for improved throughput.

Approach: the cohorter computes a length-affinity vector for each request based on requested max_tokens, recent sequence length distribution from the request's token histogram, and a lightweight token-density predictor. Requests are then assigned to cohorts probabilistically (tempered softmax) rather than deterministically; the cohort scheduler runs an acceptance policy that weighs cohort fill-level against estimated pad cost and KV-prefill benefit. The pad-cost estimator is a tiny ridge-regression model trained on our production trace snapshots (serialized model included under benchmarks/). KV-prefill heuristics mark requests with hot-key overlap to get a small scheduling boost so that cohorts end up with multiple warm-KV requests more often, reducing expensive cold-KV misses during generation. Integration points: runtime/batching/cohorter, runtime/scheduler/policy, runtime/kv/prefill, and a new bench harness under benchmarks/batch-sim.

Results: microbenchmarks on a 4xA100 pool and production-like trace show median pad tokens per request down 28% and average per-token GPU time improvement of 6% for multi-tenant traces. End-to-end P95 latency increased by 2% in worst-case stress tests but stayed within our target SLO for dedicated capacity. Cost-per-output-token decreased ~8% in the synthetic workload and ~3-5% on several customer traces. See benchmarks/summary.csv for full breakdown. We also measured improved kernel lane alignment (measured as contiguous lane occupancy metric) up 9% which maps to fewer kernel launches in multi-lane kernels.

Checklist: 1) unit tests for cohorter assignment and pad-cost estimator included, 2) integration test for scheduler with KV-prefill, 3) bench harness and dataset snapshot included, 4) flag guarded rollout (feature flag: probabilistic_cohorter) and runtime config knobs (temperature, pad_cost_weight, kv_prefill_boost), 5) docs RFC added. Backwards compatibility: default feature flag is OFF; old deterministic bucketer remains unchanged unless flag enabled. No API changes.

How to test locally: run 'make bench-cohorter' to reproduce microbenchmarks; run integration tests 'pytest tests/integration/test_cohorter.py::TestPrefillBias'. Deployment plan: staged rollout behind feature flag; enable for 5% of traffic on dedicated clusters for 48 hours, validate P95 and pad metrics in console dashboards; increase to 100% if stable.

Related: follows from ENG-7123 (experimental pad-cost model) and complements PRs for kv-prefill routing. Linear: ENG-8852. Notes: tuned default temperature=0.4 and pad_cost_weight=0.7 after ablations; conservative defaults chosen to avoid regressing latency-sensitive customers.

title: Epochal hotpath bisector and phase-stress harness for p95 reproduction
preview:
Motivation: We've seen recurring intermittent p95 regressions across long-running multi-epoch workloads where the signal only appears after many warmup cycles. Root-cause is often a narrow commit window interacting with kernel scheduler behaviour and warmup timing. This PR introduces two complementary tools: (1) an epochal hotpath bisector that runs compact workload epochs across commit ranges to quickly narrow offending commits, and (2) a phase-stress harness that injects controlled scheduling/jitter/prefetch delays into the runtime to reliably amplify the rare p95 event so it becomes reproducible in CI/local benches.

What this change does: Implements a bisector CLI that (a) shards an epochal workload into deterministic micro-epochs, (b) runs them across a git commit range with parallel workers and conservative resource isolation, and (c) aggregates per-epoch phase-bucket histograms to highlight commit-correlated shifts. Adds a phase-stress harness that can shim the runtime with configurable preemption latency, simulated kernel prefetch stalls, and induced KV-cache fragmentation to stress the hotpath under controlled conditions. Also adds a compact serializer (phase-bucket format) and a small reporting pipeline that generates a delta summary highlighting shifted quantiles and hotpath stack tags.

How to run (short): 1) Install bench Docker image: docker build -f Dockerfile.bench -t rwl-bench:phase . 2) Run bisector: bench/bisect --start <commit> --end <commit> --epochs 50 --workers 6 --mode phase-stress --stress-config bench/phase_stress/config.yaml 3) Inspect reports in benchmarks/reporting/<run-id>.phase.json and open docs/benchmarking/hotpath-bisector.md for guidance.

Checklist: - [x] Bisector core CLI, control plane, and parallel runner - [x] Phase-stress harness with 3 stress primitives (preemption, prefetch stall, kv-frag) - [x] Phase-bucket serializer and delta reporter - [x] Unit tests for serializer and small integration test for harness - [x] CI workflow to run a fast smoke bench on PRs - [x] Docs + example primer JSON - [ ] Long-run nightly job wiring (follow-up ENG-1708)

Testing and results: Local reproduction: used a historical regression window and reduced the commit range from ~2k commits to a 6-commit candidate using the bisector in ~45 minutes (6 workers, 50 epochs). Phase-stress amplified the original intermittent p95 from ~850ms to a stable 1.4–1.8s under the stress profile used in the reproduction (config included). CI: fast smoke run in PR pipeline completes within ~12 minutes (benchmarks.yml) and all checks passed after two re-runs on flaky network-injected step.

Limitations and risk: This is experimental and intentionally feature-flagged. The phase-stress primitives are intrusive (they modify scheduling hooks in the bench runtime) and can perturb other telemetry; their use should be limited to debug runs. Bisector increases CI compute usage when run across large commit ranges; the default parallelism is conservative.

Rollout plan: merge gated under label "experimental" and enable for internal SRE+perf teams. Nightly long-run job wiring and a small Linear task to auto-create candidate PRs when a bisect run identifies a single offending commit is tracked under ENG-1708.

Follow-ups: (1) Add automatic regression-gist generation for use in alert tickets (ENG-1642), (2) add an option to replay bisector rundata against dedicated GPU pools, (3) improve phase-bucket visualization in Console.

Contact: ava@redwood.example for bisector CLI questions and miguel@redwood.example for harness details.

title: introduce adaptive sensitivity scheduler for canary detectors
preview:
Motivation: long-running canary evaluators are sensitive to traffic volatility and changing baseline variance; fixed detection thresholds either generate noisy false positives during churn or miss subtle regressions when traffic is stable. This PR implements an Adaptive Sensitivity Scheduler (ASS) that continuously tunes detector sensitivity based on short-term traffic variance, cohort churn, and historical false-positive rate.

What it does (high level): introduces a scheduler that adjusts detector thresholds and aggregation windows at runtime. The scheduler observes three signals: instantaneous request-rate volatility, percentile drift across 1/5/15-minute windows, and the detector's empirical false-positive count. Using a lightweight Bayesian update, it adapts the detection sensitivity multiplier and the sample window size with safety bounds to avoid oscillation. Integrations added: canary evaluator policy (gates sensitivity changes behind rollout guard), telemetry hooks for new metrics (sensitivity_multiplier, scheduled_window_sec, scheduler_confidence), and opt-in feature-flagging via canary config.

Key design points: (1) Conservative priors: initial sensitivity is a small multiple of baseline to prevent overreaction after deploy spikes. (2) Stability hysteresis: changes are rate-limited and smoothed with exponential decay to avoid flip-flopping when traffic oscillates. (3) Safety gates: sensitivity cannot exceed an upper bound derived from SLO burn-rate budgets; if upper bound is reached scheduler forces immediate pause and raises a canary-alert event. (4) Auditability: scheduler emits human-readable decisions and rationale to the canary run log for postmortems.

Files & code: new scheduler implementation lives in perf-canary/scheduler/adaptive_scheduler.py with a lightweight C++-backed hot path for real-time metrics aggregation (perf-canary/scheduler/fastagg.cc) and Python bindings. Canary policy integrations are in perf-canary/evaluator/policy.py with new hooks to consult scheduled sensitivity before scoring. Configuration schema is extended: perf-canary/config/schema.yaml and charts/canary-helm/values.yaml include feature flag and bounds. Unit and integration tests added under tests/test_adaptive_scheduler.py and tests/integration/canary_scheduler_flow.test.

Testing and benchmarks: ran synthetic workloads (steady, bursty, seasonal) across canary clusters. Observed 47% reduction in false-positive rate on bursty traffic, with less than 3% decrease in true-positive detection rate for sustained regressions. Latency overhead on critical path measured <0.3ms per evaluation due to the fastagg hot path. Added CI job that runs targeted scheduler unit tests and a small end-to-end canary simulation (simulated 10k requests).

Rollout plan: feature gated off by default. Begin canary rollout to internal perf-canary staging clusters (10% of internal traffic) for 48h, monitor sensitivity_multiplier, scheduled_window_sec and scheduler_confidence metrics. If metrics look stable (no increase in false-positive rate and no sustained sensitivity upper bound hits), enable for production read-only (telemetry only) then enable active mode after one week. Provided a rollback script and documented runbook in perf-canary/ops/runbooks/ASS-runbook.md.

Why unique: unlike prior work which focused on fixed heuristics or static traffic-shape profiles, this scheduler continuously adapts to observed variance and links sensitivity to SLO budget. It is tailored for real-world LLM inference traffic where short-lived bursts and long-tail shifts are common.

Notes: this PR intentionally keeps the scheduler implementation modular so it can be replaced with a learned policy later (see ENG-7402). Backwards compatibility: canary config defaults preserve existing behavior when the feature is disabled.

title: Add quantile-weighted, phase-aware throughput fence and severity scoring
preview:
Problem: Existing canary detectors surface aggregate latency shifts but often miss multi-phase regressions where different traffic slices degrade at different times. We need a detector that (1) understands traffic phase boundaries (time-of-day, model warmup phases, routing slices) and (2) produces a single severity score that reflects both quantile skew and the persistence of the shift so rollout guards can make informed decisions.

This PR introduces a quantile-weighted, phase-aware throughput fence and a severity scoring function that folds into the canary evaluator pipeline. The detector computes quantile deltas per phase window and applies an exponential decay weighting across recent windows to emphasize sustained regressions while suppressing short-lived bursts. The severity score is a monotonic mapping of weighted-quantile deltas to [0,100] and is used by policy engines to trigger soft-fallback, pause, or hard-rollback depending on thresholds.

Key design points:
- Phase detection: configurable sliding window with heuristic phase boundary markers (traffic-rate change, model variant switch, time-partitioning). Phase rules are pluggable and default to traffic-rate heuristic.
- Quantile weighting: compare target percentiles (p50/p90/p99) against baseline, compute signed deltas, and transform them with a per-quantile sensitivity factor; combine into a single delta vector.
- Temporal aggregation: recent phase deltas are aggregated with exponential decay alpha (configurable) to compute sustained score.
- Severity mapping: configurable breakpoints to map numeric score to severity levels (info/warning/critical).
- Integration: detector implements the Detector interface and plugs into the existing canary evaluator; no changes required to routing or rollout policies other than optional configuration knobs.

Why different: unlike percentile-fusion detectors that treat traffic as a single homogeneous stream, this approach explicitly segments by phase and weights quantile violations to produce a severity that more closely tracks user-visible impact and avoids noisy short-lived triggers.

Behavioral knobs added in values and config:
- phase_window_seconds (default 300)
- lookback_phase_count (default 8)
- decay_alpha (default 0.4)
- quantile_weights: map {50:0.2, 90:0.5, 99:0.3}
- severity_thresholds: {warning: 25, critical: 60}

Testing and verification:
- unit tests for quantile delta calc, decay aggregation, and severity mapping included.
- local canary lab harness run against synthetic traffic with phase shifts (see test fixtures) demonstrating >92% detection rate for sustained 30% p90 regressions and <6% false positive rate on bursty noise.
- performance: detector adds ~1.3% CPU overhead in our canary agent under baseline load and 4MB additional RSS per agent.

Migration: default configuration preserves current canary behavior; operators can opt-in by enabling the detector through perf-canary config. Documentation added (docs/design/quantile-weighted-detector.md) and chart values exposed for helm deployments.

Checklist: 
- [x] Implementation of detector and integration with evaluator pipeline
- [x] Unit tests and CI coverage
- [x] End-to-end canary lab runs with synthetic traffic profiles
- [x] Documentation and helm values
- [x] Telemetry: new metrics prefixed with perf_canary.qw_ for score, phase_count, and per-quantile-delta

Related: SUP-4012 incident follow-up that motivated phase awareness; ENG-2145 for long-running canary reliability improvements.

Notes: This change is additive and opt-in. I expect additional tuning of quantile_weights and decay_alpha after broader rollout.

title: Unify latency-aware retry profiles and add lightweight StreamOperator shim across Py/TS/Go SDKs
preview: Motivation: Customers running inference across unstable networks (mobile/edge) see bursty p95/p99 spikes that cause suboptimal retry decisions (either aggressive retries causing overload or conservative backoffs hurting latency). This PR introduces a latency-aware retry profile that adapts backoff floors using short-term p95 latency windows and exposes a small StreamOperator shim to normalize streaming cancelation / graceful-drain semantics across Python, TypeScript, and Go SDKs. Summary of changes: 1) Add network-aware retry profiles (LatencyAwarePolicy) and a lightweight metrics window used by the retry controller. 2) Introduce StreamOperator: a minimal cross-language adapter that provides Pause/Resume/Abort semantics and a consistent onClose reason propagation so SDKs behave identically when servers send partial error frames or mid-stream reauth redirects. 3) Ergonomic wrappers: Python convenience helpers (backoff presets + decorator), TS fluent builder for retry policies, and Go factory method for embedding LatencyAwarePolicy into existing client constructors. 4) Parity fixes: ensure session headers (x-rw-session, x-rw-lease) and retry metadata are forwarded on reconnect paths; align TypeScript streaming event envelope with Python/Go. 5) Docs, end-to-end integration tests, and microbenchmarks added. Compatibility notes: - Non-breaking by default: existing retry behaviors remain the same unless a LatencyAwarePolicy is explicitly enabled. - SDK version bumps: python -> 1.14.0, typescript -> 1.27.0, go -> 1.8.2. - No protocol changes to the server; shim operates client-side. Testing & validation: - Unit tests for policy decision logic and StreamOperator state machine included. - Integration tests simulate packet loss, reauth frames, and slow tails. - Benchmarks show median cost per token unchanged, p95 latency reduced 6-11% under simulated 10-20% packet loss. Rollout plan: - Canary enablement in Redwood Dedicated cluster with runtime flag for 2 weeks. - Gradual opt-in for Hosted API customers via SDK flag. - Telemetry: added retry_policy.name, retry.window_ms, stream.operator.events to debug traces. Checklist: - [x] Implementation in py/ts/go SDKs - [x] Unit + integration tests - [x] Docs + examples - [x] Benchmarks and CI green - [x] SDK version bumps and changelogs

title: split-heavy-integration-tests-add-incremental-test-runner-and-dep-gate
preview:
Motivation: Our nightly and PR CI pipelines are dominated by a small set of heavy integration tests that run even when irrelevant files change. This PR: 1) splits heavy integration tests into an explicit heavy-integration job that only runs when infra/runtime or integration test files change (pattern: tests/integration/**, infra/**, runtime/**) 2) introduces an incremental test runner called 'fast-checker' which runs unit tests and a lightweight set of integration smoke tests for most PRs; it uses a file change heuristic to determine coverage 3) adds a dependency viability gate that runs a quick dependency-check step (build-profiler + dep-heuristic) on PRs that touch package manifests or lockfiles and fails the gating stage if a dependency update increases binary size or adds native toolchain requirements beyond configured thresholds 4) augments release automation to defer tag creation until required smoke tests and cost-sanity checks pass (prevents noisy release tags from incomplete CI).

Checklist:
- [x] implement fast-checker incremental runner and integrate with existing jest/runtests
- [x] add heavy-integration job and change patterns
- [x] wire dep-heuristic step and build-profiler binary size check
- [x] update docs/ci.md and README workflow section
- [x] add unit tests for the file-matching heuristics
- [x] smoke-run in staging CI

Commits summary:
- 8 commits: initial fast-checker runner scaffolding; integration job split; dep-heuristic binary-size probe; build-profiler tool (small shim); docs update; test heuristics and unit tests; CI workflow adjustments; fix follow-up from review.

Changed areas (high-level):
- .github/workflows/ci.yml: split jobs into fast-checker, heavy-integration, dep-viability
- tools/build-profiler/: small binary-size probe used by dep-heuristic
- infra/ci/fast-checker/: runner scripts and node harness to select test subsets
- scripts/ci/dep-heuristic.sh: runs quick install, runs build-profiler and verifies thresholds
- docs/ci.md: CI behavior and gating rules

Illustrative snippets (small):
- File-matching heuristic used by fast-checker (in infra/ci/fast-checker/select.sh): "git diff --name-only $BASE..$HEAD | grep -E '(^tests/integration/|^infra/|^runtime/|^package.json|^yarn.lock)' || true"
- build-profiler usage in dep-heuristic: "./tools/build-profiler/profile.sh dist/main.js --report-json > /tmp/profile.json"

Why this approach: We want the common fast-path (iterative development) to be fast without changing test quality. Heavy runs still exist for PRs that touch relevant areas and for nightly jobs. The dependency gate reduces release thrash from inadvertent large binary or native deps sneak-ins.

title: Presubmit: introduce smart-delta change detector and release-quiesce adaptor
preview:
Motivation: our presubmit pipeline is expensive and noisy for small documentation/typo changes and adds latency to fast patches. Separately, release automation occasionally collides with on-call hotfixes because we lack a short quiesce phase that gates non-critical deploys. This PR introduces two orthogonal improvements in a single, low-risk rollout: 1) a lightweight smart-delta detector (smart-delta) that classifies PRs into minimal-presubmit / full-presubmit lanes based on changed paths and simple AST heuristics; 2) a release-quiesce adaptor that allows the release pipeline to declaratively request a short quiesce window (default 5m) during which non-urgent merges are delayed until a safety window is cleared.

Summary of changes:
- new pkg/presubmit/smartdelta: change detector service + CLI used by CI to select presubmit profile
- presubmit-profiles.yaml: adds minimal, fast, and full profiles with explicit step lists
- CI: changes to .github/workflows/presubmit.yaml to consult smartdelta and route to fast lanes when eligible
- tooling: toolcache priming for fast-lane images and a small Go shim to avoid long Python cold-starts
- release: small hook in release-orchestrator that honors release-quiesce requests and emits telemetry to ci/telemetry
- docs: docs/presubmit.md and docs/releases/quiesce.md with usage and opt-out guidance

Checklist:
- [x] design doc (Linear ENG-1934) linked
- [x] unit tests for smart-delta heuristics
- [x] e2e smoke in dry-run mode
- [x] canary enabled behind feature flag
- [x] audit of security implications
- [x] documentation and migration notes

Migration notes: This ships behind a feature flag (presubmit.smart_delta) defaulting to off. Operations can enable gradually per-team. The release-quiesce adaptor requires the release manager client to add a one-line annotation to release manifests; behavior is backward-compatible when the annotation is absent.

Commits:
- 7 commits: "add smartdelta classifier and basic heuristics", "wire smartdelta into presubmit workflow", "fast-lane build image priming and caching shim", "introduce release quiesce adaptor and telemetry", "presubmit profiles and docs", "unit tests and e2e dry-run smoke", "small refactor: decouple path rules from build profiles"

Files changed (high-level):
- pkg/presubmit/smartdelta/classifier.go
- pkg/presubmit/smartdelta/rules.go
- cmd/smartdelta/main.go (CLI used by CI)
- .github/workflows/presubmit.yaml (conditional routing)
- scripts/toolcache/prime-fastlane.sh
- images/fast-lane/Dockerfile (small priming image)
- infra/release-orchestrator/quiesce_adaptor.go
- docs/presubmit.md
- docs/releases/quiesce.md
- tests/presubmit/smartdelta_test.go
- testdata/presubmit/samples/*

Notes on implementation: smart-delta uses a deterministic, fast heuristic first: changed file globs -> short-circuit minimal presubmit; for ambiguous cases it parses lightweight AST fragments (Go/TS/Python) to detect exported signature changes or dependency edits. We intentionally avoid running full static analysis to keep classification <150ms per PR. The release quiesce adaptor exposes an API endpoint in the release orchestrator that accepts a quiesce request (release-id + duration + reason). The pipeline honors the request by postponing merges on non-critical lanes and emitting telemetry to ci/telemetry with a structured event (quiesce.requested/quiesce.released).

Security/operational considerations:
- Smart-delta is conservative by default (prefers full-presubmit on uncertainty) to avoid missed regressions.
- CLI runs in ephemeral CI container with read-only access; it does not execute repository code.
- Telemetry contains only PR metadata and feature flags; no source contents are shipped externally.

Testing and rollout plan:
- Alpha: enable for docs-only teams for 2 weeks; monitor false-negative rate via presubmit-labels and run parallel full-presubmit for a sampling of PRs.
- Beta: broaden to internal infra teams, enable fast-lane image priming, and measure median presubmit latency.
- GA: enable per-repo by default with opt-out via presubmit.config. Rollout is gated by false-negative threshold <0.5% and CI pass rate parity.

Review conversation: "Ava Chen: overall nice. Can you add a guard to prevent smartdelta running on WIP/draft PRs?" -> "Sofia Martens: good call, added draft detection in CLI and unit tests (see commit 4)." "Miguel Torres: the AST path detection seems Go/TS/Python only — ok to land, but please add a clear extension point for Rust/Java in rules.go" -> "Sofia Martens: added interface and documented extension steps in rules.go comments." "Priya Nair: CI changes seem safe but please add a fallback in presubmit.yaml if smartdelta binary fails (so we don't block merges)." -> "Sofia Martens: added retry + fallback to full-presubmit; also added instrumentation to track binary failures."

CI summary:
- GitHub Actions: presubmit (fast-lane smoke) -> passed
- unit (pkg/presubmit) -> passed
- integration (dry-run) -> passed
- lint -> passed
- release-orchestrator integration -> passed

Merge: merged via squash-and-merge after approvals (Ava Chen, Miguel Torres) and green CI.

Post-merge actions:
- Turn feature flag on for docs-team in staging for canary (ops ticket created: SUP-442)
- Announce in #devops and add short runbook for release managers on how to request quiesce windows
- Create telemetry dashboard (CI team) with quiesce events and smartdelta routing rates

title: Introduce stage-aware merge prober and slimline tooling shim
preview:
Context and motivation: Merges into main occasionally surface release-hook regressions or flaky lint/test failures that only appear in the full release smokeflow. This PR introduces a lightweight, stage-aware 'merge-prober' that runs a short, cost-controlled set of checks before allowing a squash/merge and refactors the existing monolithic lint/test runner into a plugin adapter to make fast-path lanes pluggable and easier to maintain. The goal is to shorten merge feedback loops and reduce post-merge rollbacks without adding heavy CI latency.
Changes at a glance: 1) New merge-prober service and GH Actions job that performs staged checks (configurable by repo labels); 2) Refactor lint/test runner into a plugin interface, extract Node/Go/TS plugins; 3) Bump a handful of GitHub Actions and pin SHAs for reproducibility; 4) Remove legacy scripts in scripts/ci-legacy and migrate their small, relied-on bits into the plugin adapter; 5) Update docs and the release checklist.
Checklist: - [x] Design doc reviewed (ENG-2149) - [x] Tests for the merge-prober (unit + smoke harness) - [x] Plugin adapter integration smoke runs - [x] Action SHAs pinned and lockfile updated - [x] Docs updated under CONTRIBUTING.md
Migration notes: The merge-prober is opt-in per repo via a .merge-prober.yaml file; the default policy is 'fast' which performs lint subset + unit test selectors + release-hook dryrun; teams can opt into 'strict' for additional integration checks. The plugin adapter exposes a minimal lifecycle API: prepare -> run -> report which allows lightweight per-language plugins to implement fast heuristics (e.g., TypeScript uses transitive changed-file list + tsserver diagnostics; Go uses short-cmd 'go vet' + unit test selector).
Rollout plan: staged toggles on a few low-risk repos for 2 weeks, then platform-wide opt-in, with rollback switch available in the console.
Commits summary: see commit_summaries field for per-commit bullets.
Changed areas: high-level changed_files list included below.
CI and validation: initial rollout ran nightly smoke on a fork; ephemeral runners used for cost control; CI matrix reduced to a single fast lane for the prober and secondary full-pipeline checks.
Performance: average additional pre-merge latency ~18s on warm runners; rate-limited prober runs to prevent overload.
Security: action SHAs pinned; no change to secrets handling.
Merge outcome: merged to main via squash, release noted in changelog.
Why different from prior PRs: Instead of only changing CI templates or cache keys, this PR adds a lightweight runtime component (merge-prober) paired with a structural refactor (plugin adapter) to reduce long-term maintenance cost and support per-repo policy. If issues are observed we can toggle the prober off via repo config.

title: Coordinate cross-SDK credential surface and streaming ergonomics
preview:
Motivation: Multiple SDK teams (Python/TypeScript/Go) have drifted in credential APIs and streaming ergonomics: mismatch in session factory shapes, differing retry preset names, and inconsistent stream iterator behaviour caused duplicated adaptation logic in upstream services. This PR coordinates a minimal, backwards-compatible convergence across the three SDKs by introducing a small cross-language contract (documented in sdk/README/SESSION.md), consolidating retry preset names, and adding a lightweight token-warmup hook for stream-first requests.

What this PR does (high level): 1) Introduces SessionFactory v2 surface (polyglot contract) and a thin compatibility shim on top of existing SessionFactory implementations to avoid mass migrations. 2) Adds a RetryProfile alias map so \"default\"/\"standard\"/\"balanced\" map to a canonical engine preset at runtime and surfaces the mapping in SDK logs. 3) Stabilizes stream consumer ergonomics: async generator/pull-stream implementations now expose a canonical .checkpoint() method and consistent error wrapping for transient auth/IO failures. 4) Adds a token_warmup hook that allows SDKs to prefetch tokens for zero-first-byte latency on long-running streams. 5) Bumps SDK versions: python-sdk 1.12.0, typescript-sdk 0.9.7, go-sdk v1.5.3 and updates changelogs.

Compatibility and migration notes: The SessionFactory v2 is opt-in via the configuration flag \"enable_session_v2\". The compatibility shim will log a single deprecation warning when enabled against older server-side contracts. Retry preset aliases are additive (no removed names). The only behavioural change that could affect callers is the standardized stream error envelope (wraps transient errors as StreamTransientError). A migration doc with examples and copy/paste fixes is included and linked in release notes.

Testing and verification: Unit tests added across all three SDK test suites (python: tests/test_session_v2.py, ts: test/stream-checkpoint.spec.ts, go: session/factory_test.go). End-to-end smoke runs performed in the staging cluster: stream warmup reduced median TTFB by ~7% on synthetic workloads; no increased error rate on 48-hour soak.

Checklist: - [x] Design doc updated (sdk/README/SESSION.md) - [x] Unit tests for new surfaces - [x] E2E smoke on staging - [x] Changelog and version bumps - [x] Release notes draft - [x] Harmony with SDK owners (see reviewers)

Related: ENG-2134 (credential parity), ENG-2139 (stream ergonomics), PM-501 (release coordination). Links: design doc: https://linear.app/redwood/issue/ENG-2134/session-v2-cross-sdk, staging run summary: https://internal.ci/redwood/stage/178945/run-summary

title: operator credential bakeoff: add KMS provenance proxy and RBACv2 residency sentinel
preview:
Background and motivation: We routinely see two operational risks during maintenance windows and operator actions: 1) operator credentials getting reused across regions leading to ambiguous KMS telemetry, and 2) audit logs that lack strong, tamper-evident provenance tying a request to the exact KMS path used. This PR introduces a lightweight, opt-in 'credential bakeoff' flow that 1) proxies KMS calls to capture provenance assertions, 2) records an immutable provenance token in the audit ledger, and 3) performs RBACv2 shadow-checks (residency sentinel) before approving cross-region operator actions. The goal is to enable safe testing of stricter policies without immediate enforcement.

What this PR does (high level):
- Add internal/kms/provenance_proxy: a sidecar-like HTTP proxy component that intercepts KMS calls from operator tooling, validates upstream KMS responses, and emits a signed provenance token (HMAC over request metadata + key id + region) used by audit ingestion.
- Add pkg/audit/epoch_validator: immutably anchors provenance tokens into the audit ledger and rejects duplicate provenance tokens for the same operator session.
- Add pkg/rbac/sentinel/residency_shadow.go: RBACv2 sentinel that runs a dry-run decision for residency-related grants and annotates traces with the predicted policy decision. Runs in shadow mode behind feature flag.
- Expose helm values and a small console toggle to opt a cluster into the bakeoff flow: security.operator_bakeoff_v1.enabled = true
- Add unit/integration tests and an e2e scenario that simulates cross-region operator actions and verifies provenance tokens appear in the audit index.

Design notes and non-goals:
- This is intentionally opt-in and non-blocking by default. The sentinel runs in shadow mode (no deny) until rollout plans in the security Linear ticket ENG-3512 move us to enforcement.
- We sign provenance tokens using the same KMS-backed key used for audit envelopes; the proxy adds an extra request-level HMAC to link caller identity, timestamp, and KMS key fingerprint.
- We do not change legacy audit ingestion for customers who do not enable the bakeoff.

Checklist:
- [x] Add provenance proxy service and lightweight request schema
- [x] Add epoch validator for audit ledger
- [x] Add RBACv2 shadow sentinel and tracing hooks
- [x] Add helm values and quickstart note in console
- [x] Unit tests (pkg/rbac, pkg/audit, internal/kms)
- [x] Integration test against a local KMS emulator
- [x] Documentation draft (docs/security/operator-bakeoff.md)

Commits (summary):
- 58c2f9c: initial provenance proxy implementation and tests
- 3b7a1d2: audit epoch validator and ledger anchor API
- 9f4d2aa: residency shadow sentinel + policy wiring
- a7c1be8: helm chart values + console feature toggle
- f2d6e71: e2e test and CI flake fixes

Files touched (high level):
- internal/kms/provenance_proxy.go
- pkg/audit/epoch_validator.go
- pkg/rbac/sentinel/residency_shadow.go
- deploy/helm/redwood/values-security.yaml
- docs/security/operator-bakeoff.md
- tests/e2e/operator_bakeoff_residency_test.go
- go.mod / go.sum updates for jose/hmac helper libs

Testing and CI:
- Local unit tests: pkg/rbac (56 tests), pkg/audit (18 tests), internal/kms (12 tests) — all passing locally.
- CI: Build (pass), Unit (pass), Integration (pass on retry), Lint (pass), Security-scan (pass). One intermittent integration test (TestKMSProxyRetry) failed on first run due to emulator timeout; test retried and passed on second run. CI artifacts attached.

text:
Background and motivation: We routinely see two operational risks during maintenance windows and operator actions: 1) operator credentials getting reused across regions leading to ambiguous KMS telemetry, and 2) audit logs that lack strong, tamper-evident provenance tying a request to the exact KMS path used. This PR introduces a lightweight, opt-in 'credential bakeoff' flow that 1) proxies KMS calls to capture provenance assertions, 2) records an immutable provenance token in the audit ledger, and 3) performs RBACv2 shadow-checks (residency sentinel) before approving cross-region operator actions. The goal is to enable safe testing of stricter policies without immediate enforcement.

What this PR does (high level):
- Add internal/kms/provenance_proxy: a sidecar-like HTTP proxy component that intercepts KMS calls from operator tooling, validates upstream KMS responses, and emits a signed provenance token (HMAC over request metadata + key id + region) used by audit ingestion.
- Add pkg/audit/epoch_validator: immutably anchors provenance tokens into the audit ledger and rejects duplicate provenance tokens for the same operator session.
- Add pkg/rbac/sentinel/residency_shadow.go: RBACv2 sentinel that runs a dry-run decision for residency-related grants and annotates traces with the predicted policy decision. Runs in shadow mode behind feature flag.
- Expose helm values and a small console toggle to opt a cluster into the bakeoff flow: security.operator_bakeoff_v1.enabled = true
- Add unit/integration tests and an e2e scenario that simulates cross-region operator actions and verifies provenance tokens appear in the audit index.

Design notes and non-goals:
- This is intentionally opt-in and non-blocking by default. The sentinel runs in shadow mode (no deny) until rollout plans in the security Linear ticket ENG-3512 move us to enforcement.
- We sign provenance tokens using the same KMS-backed key used for audit envelopes; the proxy adds an extra request-level HMAC to link caller identity, timestamp, and KMS key fingerprint.
- We do not change legacy audit ingestion for customers who do not enable the bakeoff.

title: add-invoke-slice-sampler-and-token-cascade-explainer
preview: Motivation: long-tail token cost and latency noise make it hard to correlate bursts to kernel warmup, prefix vs completion cost, and scheduling contention. This PR introduces two complementary observability primitives: (1) an Invoke Slice Sampler that slices invocations into time-aligned 'slices' to preserve bursty token-attribution while dramatically reducing cardinality and storage cost; and (2) a Token Cascade Explainer that attributes per-token cost/latency to a small set of causal contributors (prefix KV hits/misses, kernel warmup penalty, batching wait, and I/O stalls) and exposes an explainer panel in the console. Summary of changes: - New sampler: telemetry/ingest/slice_sampler.go adds a deterministic slice keyed by (tenant, route, invocation_bucket) and emits sampled span anchors plus compact slice digests. Sampler is opt-in via telemetry.config.slice_sampling.enabled=false|true. - Token cascade: pkg/observability/token_cascade.go implements cascade attribution heuristics that correlate per-token timing with kernel occupancy metadata and KV cache events; exposes cascade tags on spans (cascade.stage and cascade.score). - Dashboards: dashboards/invocation_cascade_explainer.json adds a new panel with per-slice token waterfall, kernel warmup heatmap, and slice-level cost breakdown (prefix vs completion vs kernel). - Ingest/transform: telemetry/transform/slice_hydrator.go and telemetry/transform/cascade_enricher.go perform lightweight join of sampled anchors to full spans to produce rollups. - Docs & examples: docs/observability/token-cascade.md and examples/observability/quickstart-slice.sh. - Tests: tests/e2e/observability/synthetic_slice_test.py adds deterministic workload that reproduces warmup-burst patterns and validates attribution. Design notes and rollout: the slice sampler reduces telemetry volume by ~12x on our internal bursty trace workloads; we configured a conservative default sampling rate of 1/100 slices per tenant but added adaptive rate increases for tenants with high SLO sensitivity. The cascade explainer is purely additive instrumentation and will appear as optional panels; it does not change any runtime decision logic. Backwards compatibility: existing span shapes and tags remain unchanged unless telemetry.config.cascade_enrichment.enabled=true. Rollout plan: feature flag gated, staged rollout over 2 weeks: opt-in in staging, 10% of production tenants, 50% after stability checks, full rollout. Testing: unit tests for sampler determinism, integration tests for hydration path, and e2e synthetic replay. Checklist: - [x] implement sampler and hydrator - [x] add cascade attribution heuristics - [x] new dashboard panels and docs - [x] synthetic e2e tests - [x] feature-flag gating and config defaults Performance notes: measured ~0.8ms median overhead added to ingest path when enrichment enabled (95th percentile spikes at 5ms during backfill). Storage cost impact: with default sampling we saw ~12x reduction in sampled-span volume on internal traces, with cascade rollups adding ~3% storage. Related: ENG-6591 (observability long-tail), SUP-4121 (customer-reported burst attribution request).

title: shortburst-attention-fusion-and-slab-packing-for-short-sequence-latency
preview: Motivation: Many customer workloads at Redwood are dominated by short sequences (<64 tokens) where per-request overhead (kernel launch, KV cache management, micro-batch alignment) dominates token cost. This PR introduces a runtime path optimized for short-burst workloads: 1) an attention-fusion kernel variant that fuses small-sequence attention + rotary/ALiBi precompute to reduce kernel launches for sequences <= 64, 2) a token-slab packing allocator that packs multiple short requests into a single device slab with per-request offsets to avoid per-token KV allocations, and 3) a micro-continuous batching tweak that lowers the batching tick for short sequences while preserving overall throughput for long sequences. The goal is to reduce median latency for short requests without regressions for long-tail workloads. Changes: implemented fused_attention_short.cu and refactored attention dispatch to pick the fused kernel based on sequence_length and head_count thresholds; added slab_packer.{cc,h} which provides a fast arena allocator for short KV segments and a lightweight header describing offsets; introduced a new shortburst batching policy that lowers the flush timeout to 2ms for short requests and adds an adaptive cooldown to avoid microbursting on sustained high QPS; added smoke and microbench tests simulating tens of thousands of small requests. Compatibility: default behavior is conservative—the fused kernel engages only when model geometry matches supported head/hidden layouts and when quantization mode is FP16 or 4-bit fused path; slab packing is opt-in via runtime cfg short_slab_enabled=true to allow staged rollouts. Rollout plan: canary on dedicated-pool-3 for 48 hours, metric watch on median_p95_latency_shortseq, kv_allocator_misses, gpu_util; automatic rollback on error spikes. This PR also includes a short note for SDK teams to add a small-client-side retry jitter to avoid synchronized flapping. Checklist: [x] unit tests added for slab allocator and fused kernel dispatch [x] microbench harness for short-seq latency [x] docs: runtime tuning note in perf/short_seq.md [x] Linear ticket: ENG-1842.

title: triad-sdk-credential-window-and-stream-flow-refinements
preview: Motivation: customers reported sporadic 401s during long pull/stream sessions and awkward ergonomics for checking stream liveness. This PR introduces a lightweight credential 'sliding window' that normalizes prefetch timing across SDKs, plus two small stream ergonomics helpers (peek_end and wait_for_end) and instrumentation to correlate reauth events with stream traces. Changes span Python/TypeScript/Go SDKs and docs. What changed: 1) Credential sliding window: add a tunable lease_window and prefetch_margin to SDK credential providers so refresh is deterministic across clock skew and latency. Default profile keeps behavior identical but allows operators to reduce reauth jitter for long-lived streams. 2) Stream ergonomics: add Stream.peek_end() to poll if the upstream finished without consuming tokens, and Stream.wait_for_end(timeout) as a convenience awaitable. Implementations are fully non-blocking and optimized for the host runtime (asyncio/promises/goroutines). 3) Telemetry: emit stream.reauth.attempt and stream.reauth.success spans and attach credential_window arg for easier debugging in Console. 4) Cross-SDK parity work: align arg names and defaults across py/ts/go, bump versions (py 0.12.4 -> 0.12.5, ts 1.6.2 -> 1.6.3, go v1.5.1 -> v1.5.2). 5) Tests and docs: add unit and integration tests to cover the window edge cases and update quickstart examples and changelog. Rollout plan: feature guarded behind SDK feature flag (CRED_WINDOW_ALPHA); default is off in existing published builds so behavior is opt-in for early adopters. Impact: reduces observed 401 spikes during sustained streams, clarifies telemetry for SRE, and gives app authors ergonomic primitives for stream lifecycle. Checklist: - [x] API signatures reviewed across SDKs - [x] Telemetry schema bump registered - [x] Unit tests added - [x] Integration test with dedicated runtime - [x] Docs/quickstart updated - [x] Version bumps staged

title: Add hardware-timestamp tracepoints and synthetic-replay microbench for kernel latency and tile occupancy
preview: Motivation: pinpoint sub-kernel latency variance and per-tile occupancy skew seen during continuous batching on long sequences. This PR adds lightweight hardware-timestamp tracepoints around kernel launches, a small lock-free per-tile occupancy counter, and a deterministic synthetic-replay microbenchmark that replays recorded request traces at controlled concurrency for stable profiling. Changes are gated behind runtime flags so there is zero overhead when disabled. High-level summary: 1) runtime/tracing/hw_timestamp_trace.h/.cc implements RAII-style trace scopes that capture wallclock and GPU hw timestamps when available and annotate the internal timeline with span ids, 2) runtime/scheduler/tile_counter.h/.cc introduces a cache-friendly per-tile occupancy counter sampled by the scheduler to record occupancy distributions, 3) tools/replay_bench parses a compressed request trace and replays with configurable arrival patterns and concurrency to exercise the batching scheduler reproducibly, 4) observability/exporters/perf_dashboard_exporter.py converts trace output into a bundled JSON for the perf dashboard including per-kernel latency histograms and per-tile occupancy heatmaps, 5) added unit and microbench tests and CI job to validate reproducible runs. Checklist: - [x] add hw timestamp trace scopes - [x] implement per-tile occupancy counters - [x] implement synthetic replay bench - [x] export summarized metrics for dashboard - [x] add CI microbench job - [x] update docs and example traces. Usage notes: enable tracing with REDWOOD_TRACING=hw_timestamp or use the scheduler flag --enable-tile-counters. Replay tool accepts --trace-file and --concurrency flags. Recorded traces are compressed with a simple run-length encoding to keep artifacts small. Backwards compatibility: tracing is opt-in and defaults to off so production behavior is unchanged. Performance: measured overhead with tracing on is ~2-3% for short sequences and up to 7% during heavy kernel churn; with tracing off overhead is nil. See release notes for more details.

title: conditional eval fanout + weighted signal aggregator and CI gating
preview: Motivation: when a primary prompt-set check trips, we often need to run a set of targeted probes (e.g. hallucination prober, paraphrase-stability, token-saliency) to determine failure mode before blocking rollouts. This PR adds a conditional eval fanout layer and a weighted signal aggregator so CI can produce a single composite gating decision that reflects both severity and breadth of regressions. Design summary: - New runtime component eval_fanout orchestrates conditional parallel probes with a budget cap and backpressure to avoid blowing smoke-test windows. - WeightedSignalAggregator implements decay-weighted aggregation across cohorts and probes; weights are configurable per-linear-task and default to severity-first then coverage. - CiHook composite_slo_check integrates into existing eval CI: primary prompt-set runs as usual; on fail, fanout triggers configured probes; aggregator emits a human-readable breakdown and a single pass/fail decision. - Added JSON schema for fanout plan (configs/ : fanout-plans/*.json) and migration docs. Changes (high level): - eval/engine/fanout.py (core orchestration + retry/backoff) - eval/metrics/aggregator.py (aggregation logic, normalization) - eval/ci/composite_slo_hook.py (CI entrypoint + reporter) - configs/fanout-plans/english-and-multilingual.json - tests/{unit,integration}/test_fanout_* and smoke scenarios - docs/eval/fanout-design.md (motivation and configuration guide) Checklist: - [x] Unit tests covering core aggregator math and scheduling heuristics - [x] Integration smoke that runs fanout on a small sample prompt set within CI budget - [x] CI gating rule added and tested in staging - [x] Backwards-compatible configs (existing eval runs unchanged unless plan specified) Rollout plan: merge to main, enable composite_slo_check behind feature-flag per-repo, monitor eval/telemetry.composite_gate.* metrics for two weeks, then flip default for canaries. Related: ENG-2048 for production tuning, ENG-2110 for policy UI knobs. Notes: kept probes as light-weight callable adapters so adding new probes doesn't require changing orchestration core. See internal doc: https://linear.app/redwood/board/ENG-2048/fanout-spec for full design.

title: introduce-cost-rules-engine-and-autotune-batching-defaults
preview: Summary: Add a Cost Rules Engine that automatically recommends and applies batching and KV-cache defaults per model and workload profile, exposes quantization profile hints for safe auto-application, and introduces a unit-economics estimator in the console and CLI. Motivation: customers routinely ask for hands-off cost tuning; operational teams want reproducible, audited defaults that reduce per-token costs without surprising latency regressions. This PR bundles runtime heuristics, a small rules DSL, console UI plumbing, and an opt-in rollout for auto-quantization. What changed (high level): - new runtime module redwood/serving/cost_rules.py with rules evaluation, telemetry hooks, and safe-apply gates - batcher tuning extension that selects default batch sizes based on latency SLOs and sequence-length histograms - KV cache policy defaults and prefix cache eligibility heuristics - quantization profiling pipeline (offline profiler + runtime hinting) and a safe auto-apply policy with rollback - console/CLI cost estimator showing projected per-token unit economics with suggested knobs - integration tests, docs, and an opt-in feature flag + audit logs Rollout plan: default rules are disabled by default for existing tenants. We enable the rules engine for internal canaries and add a gradual opt-in toggle in the console. Quantization auto-apply is gated behind a policy that requires: a) offline eval against golden prompt set, b) latency/regression guardrails, and c) explicit tenant opt-in. Checklist: - [x] implement rule evaluator and runtime hooks - [x] expose console estimator and API endpoints - [x] add offline quant profile pipeline and safe-apply path - [x] add unit + integration tests - [x] add docs and release notes - [x] add audit events and rollout flag Notes on safety: the rules engine never mutates a serving model binary directly; it returns a recommended configuration which a controlled subsystem applies with canarying and audits. title: Introduce presubmit-prober-lite and Oxide dep cleanser to reduce CI setup noise
preview: Context: The presubmit pipeline has grown noisy and slow due to full toolchain fetches, overly conservative devdep pinning, and repeated cold-start overheads in ephemeral CI runners. Motivation: Reduce pretest latency and flakiness by introducing a lightweight presubmit prober that warms an ephemeral 'shadow' artifact cache, a sparse-fetch wrapper to avoid pulling full toolchains for quick presubmit lanes, and a small dependency-cleanser (Oxide) that flags / optionally prunes devdeps causing large install graphs. This PR bundles a tooling + workflow refactor to give teams an opt-in fast lane for quick feedback and a hygiene tool to keep CI images and lockfiles lean. Summary of changes: - tools/presubmit-prober/: new Go binary (small, dependency-free) that probes common package registries, exercises cache layers, and warms a shadow cache on the runner prior to heavy installs. It exits 0 on successful warm, non-zero on registries unavailable (used to gate slow lanes). - tools/oxide-dep-cleanser/: Python script + policy rules that detect devdeps larger than XMB or with >N transitive deps; emits a suggested devdep rollback or a Linear ticket. Has a --dry-run mode used in CI. - .github/workflows/presubmit.yml: adds a 'quick-lint' lane that runs the prober + sparse-fetch + lint/test smoke subset for changed packages. Quick lane is opt-in via PR label "quick-feedback" and automatically triggers when change size < 200 LOC and files_changed_count < 10. - ci/ci-setup/sparse-fetch.sh: new wrapper around package managers (npm/pip/go) that uses registry list hints and fetches only declared direct deps for quick lanes. - Docker/ci: small tweaks to the CI image entrypoint to expose shadow-cache dir and make cache permissions deterministic. - docs/dev/ci-guides.md: documents opt-in criteria, observability signals, how to interpret Oxide reports, and rollout plan. Design notes: - The shadow cache is kept in the runner's ephemeral workspace and does not change upstream artifacts; it's purely advisory to reduce network and cold cache penalty. - Oxide deliberately does not edit lockfiles in CI by default; we run it with --dry-run and surface structured findings in presubmit comments. Teams can flip a repo-level config to allow automated patch PRs after manual review. - Quick-lint lane preserves strict unit/integration lanes for main pipeline; fast lane is additive and gated so it cannot replace full checks. Testing done: - Local unit tests for presubmit-prober (registry mocking), oxide (policy fuzzing) - End-to-end smoke on a canary repo with 30 sample PRs: median presubmit setup time reduced from ~125s to ~62s on quick lanes. - Integration: ran full main pipeline twice with the new hooks to ensure no regressions; a flakey integration test surfaced (test/db/timeout) which was unrelated and filed as SUP-2123. Rollout plan: - Start as opt-in for 20% of repos via repo-config opt-in label and selective enabling in .github. - Monitor presubmit latency, flake rate, and Oxide false-positive rate for 2 weeks. - Follow-up: enable auto PR creation for low-risk devdeps after product review. Checklist: - [x] Add presubmit-prober binary and tests - [x] Add Oxide script + policy rules - [x] Wire quick-lint lane and sparse-fetch wrapper - [x] Add docs and observability hooks - [x] Add reviewer feedback fixes (timeouts/logging/metrics) Related work: builds on ENG-4020 (ci sparse-fetch research) and pairs with ENG-4231 (presubmit cost telemetry).

title: introduce-telemetry-correlation-hub-and-alert-prioritizer
preview: Motivation: Customers repeatedly request better correlation between token-level cost spikes, kernel/backpressure metrics, and the sparse logs/trace fragments we capture. This PR adds a Telemetry Correlation Hub: an index + lightweight merge pipeline that synthesizes compact span digests, token-cost fingerprints, and contextual log anchors to improve triage and to drive an alert prioritizer that reduces noisy SRE toil. Design summary: - Introduce correlation index (compact-correlation-key) derived from invocation signature + first-3-token hash + kernel-shape fingerprint. - Lightweight merge step at ingestion that joins sampled spans, cost-attribution rows, and log anchors into a compact "tracelet" document stored in the telemetry store. - Alert prioritizer component that computes a per-anomaly priority score (severity*cost-impact*entropy) and attaches a reason vector used by the console to surface next-best-actions. - Dashboard additions: "Correlation Explorer" with lane view (cost, kernel-queue, span-count), and a Golden-Path heatmap for correlated alerts. - New telemetry fields: telemetry.correlation_key, telemetry.priority_score, telemetry.reason_vector (1..5 tags), and span.digest_v2 (compact base64). - No changes to public API surface; internal instrumentation only. Checklist: [x] index/key design, [x] ingestion merge pipeline, [x] prometheus/otel metrics + tags, [x] console dashboard panels, [x] alert-prioritizer rules + unit tests, [x] e2e synthetic replay smoke test, [x] rollout gating and feature flag. Migration/rollout: Feature gated behind ff.telemetry_correlation_hub; default off for Dedicated/Private. Step 1: rollout to observability-canary for 48h; Step 2: opt-in tenants; Step 3: global enable and alert-rule migration. Backwards compatibility: older traces without correlation_key remain queryable; index is best-effort and additive. Security & Privacy: correlation_key is keyed hash; log anchors are pseudonymized where required by tenant policy. Performance: ingest merge implemented with bounded window and sampling (1% global but can be increased per-tenant). Expected ingestion CPU + mem ~ +2-3% in canary. Testing: unit tests for index generation; integration tests for join correctness; replaybench runs showing <5% added p50 ingest latency in synthetic high-throughput. Commits summary: "index: add correlation_key generator and tests"; "ingest: correlation merge pipeline and schema migration"; "diag: add priority_score calculator and rule config"; "console: add Correlation Explorer panels and drilldown links"; "alerting: add alert-prioritizer and migration script"; "docs: telemetry correlation runbook and config guide". Changed files (high-level): ["services/telemetry/indexer/correlation_indexer.py","services/telemetry/pipeline/merge_stage.go","schemas/telemetry_v3.sql","observability/dashboards/correlation_explorer.json","alerting/prioritizer/rules.yaml","alerting/migrations/20260215_migrate_priorities.sql","sdk/otel/enricher.py","tests/ingest/test_correlation_indexer.py","tests/alerting/test_prioritizer.py","docs/runbooks/telemetry-correlation.md","docs/console/correlation-explorer.md","ci/replaybench/config.yaml"] Review thread summary: - Diego Martinez (requested changes 2026-02-11): "The correlation_key must avoid exposing token prefixes for PII-sensitive tenants; convert to HMAC with tenant-salt or use truncated bloom. Also add a canary toggle per-region." -> Author (2026-02-12): "Switched to HMAC-SHA256 with tenant salt, added per-region FF; updated tests." - Priya Nair (comment 2026-02-13): "Can we add a synthetic scenario that reproduces a long-tail cost spike tying kernel queueing to token splice?" -> Author (2026-02-13): "Added synthetic scenario in replaybench config and linked job in CI." - Omar Haddad (approved 2026-02-14): "Design looks sound; priority scoring config is readable and has sensible defaults." - Ops note from Alice Gupta (comment 2026-02-14): "Observability retention might increase due to priority tags; added storage budget checks and opt-outs." Author replies and fixed issues across 3 follow-up commits. CI checks: ["unit-tests: pass","integration-tests: pass","lint: pass","replaybench: pass","e2e-smoke: pass","security-scan: pass"] Merge outcome: merged via squash on 2026-02-15 after approvals and green CI. Release notes: "Add Telemetry Correlation Hub: synthesizes compact tracelets that correlate token-cost, kernel-level signals, and log anchors; includes a console Correlation Explorer and an alert prioritizer to reduce noisy alerts and improve triage. Feature is feature-flagged and disabled by default." Rollout and monitoring runbook: See docs/runbooks/telemetry-correlation.md; primary monitors added: telemetry.correlation_ingest_errors, telemetry.priority_score_dist, telemetry.tracelet_ingest_latency_p50/p95. Known limitations: correlation misses under extreme sampling; priority_score tuning will require 2 weeks of telemetry to stabilize. Future work: persistent tracelet indices for fast cross-tenant search, adaptive sampling based on priority, and alert-suppression playbook integration.
