title: Add pseudonymous token-cost summarizer and privacy-hash bridge
preview: Context/Motivation: We need per-invocation token cost visibility without retaining plaintext prompt or response tokens in observability backends. This PR introduces a privacy-first pipeline: (1) deterministic, salted token-hash bucketing at the edge; (2) a compact per-invocation cost summarizer that attributes per-bucket token counts and tokenized-costs; (3) a bridge service that joins hashed token buckets with model cost profiles for rollups and dashboards. Goals: provide accurate cost attribution for SLOs and billing heuristics while ensuring PII/PHI is never logged or exported. Changes are designed to be reversible (config toggles) and auditable. Main changes: - collector: add pseudonymization middleware that applies a per-tenant salt and BLAKE3-based chunked hashing to token fragments, exposing only bucket_id and hash_prefix_size metadata (no token text). - summarizer: a streaming aggregator that emits per-invocation compact summaries: total_tokens, generation_tokens, prompt_token_buckets (list of bucket_id:count), and model_cost_approx_usd (aggregated using model profile). - bridge service: lightweight joiner that maps bucket-level aggregates to model cost model to compute per-route and per-tenant cost metrics used by dashboards/alerts. - dashboards: add a new Observability Pack dashboard panel group 'Pseudonymous Cost Explorer' with per-route aggregated cost and a new 'quiet-surge' alert rule that triggers only when cost growth is sustained across at least 3 hours and >75th percentile of bucket skew. - telemetry: add metrics and labels for pseudonymization_version, salt_id, and summarizer_latency_ms; tracing spans annotated with sample_rate and anonymized_bucket_count. Backwards compatibility: collector can run in dual-mode emitting both legacy plaintext token telemetry (for fast migration) and pseudonymized summaries behind a feature flag. Checklist: [x] design review (ENG-4321), [x] privacy review with security, [x] unit tests for hashing and summarizer, [x] integration test with bridge, [x] dashboard screens, [x] docs draft. Migrations/deploy: - rollout plan: enable pseudonymization off-by-default; stage enable on a small subset of tenants (canary 1%), monitor summarizer latency and cost deltas for 48h, then ramp to 100% over 5 days. - rollback plan: flip feature flag to legacy mode (no schema migration required). - data retention: summarized rows kept 90d; raw hashed metadata kept 7d and then rotated. Security/privacy notes: salts are tenant-scoped and stored in KMS-backed vault; hashing performed in-process so unhashed tokens never leave the collector process. Commit summary: 1) add privacy middleware and deterministic token hashing; 2) streaming summarizer service and proto changes for compact summary; 3) bridge joiner service and query adapters for dashboards; 4) observability pack dashboard panels and alert rule; 5) e2e tests and runbook. Key files changed: observability-pack/pkg/collector/pseudonymizer.go, observability-pack/pkg/summarizer/stream_aggregator.go, observability-pack/services/bridge/joiner.go, observability-pack/dashboards/pseudonymous_cost_explorer.jsonnet, observability-pack/alerts/quiet_surge.yaml, docs/observability/privacy-token-summarizer.md, test/e2e/pseudonymizer_integration_test.go, scripts/bench/summarizer_bench.go, go.mod, go.sum. Tests: added unit tests for hash stability and collision resistance, integration test that validates summarizer output vs synthetic tokenized inputs, benchmark for 99th-pctl summarizer latency. Observability/Alerting: - New metric: pseudonymizer.tokens_hashed_total{tenant,salt_id} - New histogram: summarizer.latency_ms - New alert: observability.quiet_surge_high_cost (suppresses noisy short spikes; logic included in alert yaml) Release notes (short): Adds a privacy-preserving token-cost summarizer that allows cost attribution without storing token text; dashboards and alerts submitted with observability pack. Rollout and monitoring instructions included. Rationale and alternatives considered: we evaluated full differential privacy vs deterministic hashing; deterministic salted hashing was chosen for lower operational complexity and deterministic joins. We added salt rotation support as a safety mechanism. Open questions: - how to present per-bucket explanations to end-users without leaking token content? We opted for statistical bucket descriptors (e.g., average token length, token_type_entropy) rather than examples. - future: consider integrating cryptographic PPE techniques for smaller tenants. Observability runbook excerpt: On spike after enabling, check summarizer.latency_ms, look for high anonymized_bucket_count, and if latency > 200ms for 5m trigger rollback. Compliance: privacy review approved; KMS usage logged.

title: Ephemeral tenant shelf and circuit epoch guard
preview: Motivation: During a production incident last week we observed tenant recovery flapping when nodes with partially-drained KV caches rejoined. That flapping caused rapid circuit reopen/close cycles and triggered SLO alerts for the affected region. This PR introduces two coordinated features: 1) an ephemeral tenant-shelf that temporarily places low-priority tenant requests into a short-lived queue while the system heals, and 2) a circuit-epoch guard which binds circuit state transitions to epoch windows to prevent rapid reopen oscillations. The design aims to reduce cascade reopens, smooth SLO enforcement during regional rejoin events, and provide clear telemetry for postmortem analysis. Summary of changes: - New TenantShelf component in serving/runtime to accept and hold low-priority requests with configurable shelf TTL and max depth. - EpochGuard added to circuit-breaker state machine: transitions require an epoch stamp and minimum quiesce interval; stale epochs are rejected with clear metrics. - A new policy config (shelf.policy) to tie tenant weight, priority, and eligibility for shelving. - Graceful rollout: default disabled; enabled via runtime flag and guarded feature gate for canary. - Backward compatibility: shelf is permissive and falls back to existing behaviour if disabled. Checklist: - [x] Design doc (link: Linear ENG-4821) - [x] Unit tests for TenantShelf and EpochGuard state transitions - [x] Integration test with regional rejoin simulation - [x] Load test at 2x typical tenant burst - [x] Observability added (metrics and traces) Related work: references ENG-4803 (kv-drain coordination), ENG-4785 (tenant prioritizer). Rollout plan: merge to main behind feature gate, enable on canary region for 24h, monitor slo.delta and circuit.flip_rate for 6h, expand to other regions if stable. If regression observed, feature gate can be toggled off and a fast rollback will be performed. Notes: This PR intentionally keeps the TenantShelf in-memory for this iteration to simplify rollback; a follow-up will persist shelf metadata to avoid lost-in-flight telemetry during long drains.

Summary of changes: - New TenantShelf component in serving/runtime to accept and hold low-priority requests with configurable shelf TTL and max depth. - EpochGuard added to circuit-breaker state machine: transitions require an epoch stamp and minimum quiesce interval; stale epochs are rejected with clear metrics. - A new policy config (shelf.policy) to tie tenant weight, priority, and eligibility for shelving. - Graceful rollout: default disabled; enabled via runtime flag and guarded feature gate for canary. - Backward compatibility: shelf is permissive and falls back to existing behaviour if disabled. Checklist: - [x] Design doc (link: Linear ENG-4821) - [x] Unit tests for TenantShelf and EpochGuard state transitions - [x] Integration test with regional rejoin simulation - [x] Load test at 2x typical tenant burst - [x] Observability added (metrics and traces) Related work: references ENG-4803 (kv-drain coordination), ENG-4785 (tenant prioritizer). Rollout plan: merge to main behind feature gate, enable on canary region for 24h, monitor slo.delta and circuit.flip_rate for 6h, expand to other regions if stable. If regression observed, feature gate can be toggled off and a fast rollback will be performed. Notes: This PR intentionally keeps the TenantShelf in-memory for this iteration to simplify rollback; a follow-up will persist shelf metadata to avoid lost-in-flight telemetry during long drains.

title: SDKS: auth mosaic + streaming consolidation and ergonomic API polish
preview: Context: We observed subtle divergence in how Python/TypeScript/Go SDKs handled token refresh during long-lived streams and how retry presets influence stream resume characteristics. This caused two classes of production issues: (1) opaque reauth races that produced 401s during resume, and (2) inconsistent backpressure hints leading to latency spikes when batching heuristics changed across SDKs. This PR introduces a small cross-SDK surface (SessionSurface) and a compact "mosaic" of auth utilities to align behavior. Key goals: harmonize reauth handshake, provide ergonomic retry presets (latency-first and throughput-first), and expose minimal stream backpressure hints to SDK consumers. Changes: - Introduce SessionSurface (py) and SessionFactory (go) as a lightweight facade for long-lived sessions; exported helpers in ts for parity. - Add two retry preset bundles: latency_aware and throughput_aware (shared canonical definitions in pkg/common/retry). - Token choreography: tokenHelper/ensureToken (ts) and a small auth-mosaic helper (py/go) to centralize refresh windows and jitter. - Streaming ergonomics: backpressure hints API (ts) + iterator safety guard (go) + python convenience wrapper for async generators. - Docs & examples: migration guide with concrete recipes for upgrading long-lived streams; example showing how to pick retry presets and combine with SessionSurface. Testing: unit coverage across SDKs; manual smoke runs against a dedicated canary stack. CI: one flaky legacy test in sdks/python was rerun and passed on the second run. Checklist: - [x] API design reviews with SDK leads - [x] Cross-language parity tests - [x] Changelog entries and migration guide - [x] Version bumps for SDK packages (py 1.13.0, ts 1.29.0, go 1.12.2) Rollout plan: merge to main, cut coordinated SDK releases; library consumers that rely on automatic token refresh should switch to the new SessionSurface/Factory or call the token helper from their stream bootstrap code. Related: ENG-2345 (stream reliability), PM-7890 (developer ergonomics).

title: coordinated-rate-aware-retry-and-stream-drain-trio-sdk-bump
preview:
Context and motivation: We observed customer workloads where token quota bursts and rate limiting created poor tail latency and spurious retries across SDKs. Separately, deploys and autoscale events produced abrupt stream cancellations that surfaced as confusing errors in end-user apps. This PR implements a coordinated cross-SDK change: (1) rate-aware retry windows that scale backoff slopes and jitter based on observed success-rate and throttle signals, and (2) a graceful stream-drain/quiesce helper that allows long-lived streaming consumers to finish inflight messages before reconnecting. The goal is parity across Python, TypeScript, and Go SDKs with minimal ergonomics changes for integrators.

What changed (high level):
- Added "RateAwareRetryProfile" and default presets (conservative/throughput/aggressive). Profiles adjust maxRetryWindow, baseBackoff and jitter envelope based on recent 1m success/rate signals.
- Added streaming client method quiesce(drain_timeout_ms) + signal-based shutdown hook (client.quiesce or env REDWOOD_SDK_QUIESCE). Implemented polite shutdown handshake with server support: sends QUIT_HINT, waits for server ACK or timeout before closing the underlying transport.
- Aligned typed SDK error classes: SDKRateLimitedError, SDKStreamDrainedError, SDKTransientError with stable error.code strings ("rate_limited", "stream_drained", "transient").
- Telemetry: emit new metric sdk.retry.profile and sdk.stream.quiesce.duration to help teams tune policies.
- Bumped SDK versions and changelogs for py/ts/go.

Commits (summary):
- 0a1b2c3: add rate-aware retry library and presets (shared runtime lib)
- 1d2e3f4: implement quiesce API fo r python sdk; tests + docs
- 2f3g4h5: implement quiesce API for ts sdk; add sample in examples/streaming-quiesce
- 3h4i5j6: implement go sdk quiesce + graceful shutdown; update CI matrix
- 4j5k6l7: telemetry counters and profiling hooks; add e2e test simulator for throttles
- 5m6n7o8: bump versions and update changelogs across SDK packages

Files touched (select):
- sdk/common/retry/rate_aware_profile.go (core algorithm, small)
- sdk/python/redwood/sdk/retry.py (new RateAwareRetryProfile + mapping to client)
- sdk/python/redwood/sdk/stream.py (add Client.quiesce, tests/test_quiesce.py)
- sdk/typescript/src/retry/rateAware.ts (profile and presets)
- sdk/typescript/src/stream/quiesce.ts (client API + docs snippet)
- sdk/go/retry/profile.go (Go port + unit tests)
- examples/streaming/quiesce-demo/* (small example and README)
- docs/sdk/streaming.md (quiesce usage, best-practices)
- CHANGELOGs for py/ts/go updated

Implementation notes and trade-offs:
- We implemented rate-awareness as a lightweight sliding success-rate window; it is intentionally conservative (1m default) to avoid rapid oscillation. Teams can opt into more aggressive profiles via explicit RateAwareRetryProfile presets.
- The quiesce handshake is a best-effort signal: if server does not ACK within drain_timeout_ms, the client falls back to immediate close. This behavior was chosen to avoid blocking shutdown progress in noisy environments.
- To keep the PR approachable, the core rate-aware algorithm lives in a small shared runtime library (sdk/common/retry) and language bindings call into that when present. When the shared lib is not available (older installs), SDKs fall back to the previous fixed-profile behavior.

Review conversation (high level):
- Ava Chen: "This is a great direction — can we make the default conservative for GA? I'd rather avoid surprising customers on high-throughput workloads."
- Author (Ethan): "Agree; defaults are conservative (see presets). Added explicit test that validates no behavior change under 10 QPS unless 'throughput' preset is selected."
- Diego Morales: "Can we ensure the telemetry tags include 'profile_name' and 'active_rate_bucket' so infra teams can filter?"
- Author: "Added sdk.retry.profile tag and active_rate_bucket labels; unit tests updated to validate metric emission."
- Priya Kapoor: "One flaky e2e failed in CI on the first run (stream reconnect test). Looks like a race in the example harness."
- Author: "Yes — failure was due to the example harness not awaiting the server ACK properly. Fixed and added a short backoff in the harness. CI re-run green."
- Ava Chen: "Approved after minor doc tweak and test name clarification."

CI summary: initial CI run (linux/py38, linux/ts-node14, linux/go1.20) showed a single flaky test failure in examples/streaming (test_stream_reconnect) — root cause: harness race. We fixed the harness and re-ran. Final CI: all checks passed (unit, integration, lint).

Compatibility and rollout:
- Default behavior is backward compatible; existing apps see no change unless they upgrade SDK version.
- For Hosted customers we coordinated a server-side small flag flip to honor QUIT_HINT; rollout will be gradual in the next two weeks.
- We added a short migration note in docs and the changelog explaining the new error codes and quiesce behavior.

Backports and follow-ups (planned):
- Backport conservative preset to the 1.x maintenance branches for py/ts.
- Add an example showing how to combine quiesce with autoscaler lifecycle hooks (Kubernetes preStop).
- Explore server-side per-tenant rate signals to refine client rate buckets.

Decision/Outcome: Merged via squash on 2026-02-10 after final approvals. Version bumps included in the squash commit.

Related tickets: ENG-4219 (rate-aware retry), ENG-4220 (stream quiesce ergonomics), DOC-198 (quiesce doc updates).

Checklist: 
- [x] Implementation for Python, TypeScript, Go
- [x] Unit tests and integration tests
- [x] Telemetry and metrics instrumentation
- [x] Changelogs and docs updated
- [x] CI green after re-run

Notes for reviewers of future changes: When tuning presets, prioritize stability over raw throughput; small changes to backoff slopes can have outsized effects at scale.

title: Add release dry-run workflow, lightweight lint-prober runner, and dependency impact scanner
preview: Context: Release windows have been a recurring source of risk — releases usually exercise cross-repo hooks (changelogs, tag signing, and artifact promotion) but we lacked a reliable preflight to validate those steps and the telemetry emitted during a release. This PR introduces three coordinated pieces: (1) a release-dryrun workflow that performs a full non-mutating rehearsal of release steps in CI and emits structured telemetry; (2) a lightweight lint-prober runner that can execute a minimal subset of presubmit linters quickly on changed files (target fast feedback for large monorepo touchpoints); and (3) a dependency impact scanner that maps changed dependency versions to a quick-risk score for release gating. Motivation: reduce release surprises, shorten the pre-merge feedback loop for lint failures on large changes, and flag high-risk dependency bumps before rollout. Changes are intentionally additive and gated behind a feature flag in repo config so rollout is gradual. Checklist: - [x] Add release-dryrun workflow with non-destructive mode and artifact stubbing - [x] Emit structured telemetry to ci/release-telemetry v2 (events: dryrun.start, step.finish, manifest.diff) - [x] Implement lint-prober binary + quick rules file selector - [x] Add dep-impact scanner script with mapping table -> risk score - [x] Wire presubmit to optionally call lint-prober when change set > 200 files - [x] Unit tests for scanner heuristics and runner integration smoke tests - [x] Docs and runbook updates for on-call during dry-run rollouts Migration & rollout notes: - The release-dryrun workflow is opt-in via .github/release-config.yaml -> dryrun.enabled: true. Start by enabling for a small set of repos for one week. - Lint-prober is enabled by default for PRs touching >200 files and can be disabled using PR label ci/skip-prober. - Dep-impact scanner risk thresholds are tunable; default: score>=7 => block release; score 4-6 => warn. Monitoring: dashboards added under Console -> CI -> Release Dryruns and a new alert ci.release-dryrun.fail-rate > 0.5%/day. Why not use existing presubmit? Existing presubmit is heavy; lint-prober purposefully trades coverage for speed by running targeted rules and sampling heavy linters. Related: ENG-1423 (presubmit scaleback efforts), SUP-4121 (customer incident that motivated release rehearsals).

title: kv: granular coalesce evictor and kernel-aware routing for spill reduction
preview:
Context: We see recurrent small-fragment churn in tenant KV caches under mixed-generation workloads (short prompt inference + chat continuations). Frequent small-page evictions increase spill frequency and disk IO, which amplifies latency variance and costs. This PR implements a new eviction strategy (Granular Coalesce Evictor, GCE) combined with kernel-aware routing signals to reduce unnecessary spills and improve tail latency for production workloads.

What this PR does: Implements GCE that: (1) detects clusters of small adjacent fragments and coalesces them in-memory before choosing to spill; (2) uses a hybrid scoring function that weights fragmentation impact, tenant priority, and an estimated kernel recompile/execution cost to prefer evicting entries that are cheaper to rehydrate; (3) introduces a burst-aware coalescer that defers full compaction under sustained IO pressure. Integrates eviction signals with the kernel selector so routed requests prioritize model/kernel variants that minimize recompile overhead for likely-evicted keys.

Design notes: - Coalescing is opportunistic: we never block foreground allocations; coalesces run in background workers with adaptive throttling. - Scoring: score = w_frag*frag_score + w_tenant*priority + w_kernel*kernel_recompile_cost_estimate. Kernel cost heuristic is derived from recent kernel switch counters and sequence-length histograms. - Backpressure: the coalescer watches node-level IO/sustained-spill-rate and transitions from aggressive -> conservative -> disabled modes. - Safe-paths: in case coalescer fails or causes regressions, we fall back to previous LRU-evictor via config flag kv.evictor.mode = legacy.

Why kernel-aware routing: Small fragments often belong to short-seq workloads that map to lighter kernels; when eviction will likely occur we prefer to route new requests to kernels that avoid heavy recompile penalties. This coordination reduces overall CPU/GPU kernel churn and improves p95 for mixed workloads.

Testing and rollout: Unit and integration tests exercise scoring, fallback, and backpressure. Added bench harness in perf-canary with mixed chat + short-generation workload. Canary rollout configured: 5% -> 25% -> 100% over 72h with automated regression gate (p95 latency and spill-rate).

Notes on runtime config knobs added: - kv.evictor.coalesce_window_ms (default 200) - kv.evictor.max_coalesce_size_bytes (default 256KB) - kv.evictor.kernel_weight (default 0.4) - kv.evictor.io_backpressure_threshold (MB/s)

Related: ENG-8421 (kv memory management), ENG-9153 (kernel selection improvements).

title: Budget Savant: Prefetch Heuristics + Quant Tier Manager
preview: Context: Large tenants with bursty tail traffic waste tokens and GPU cycles during cold KV-cache windows. Goal: introduce a tenant-scoped prefilling heuristic and a quantization-tier manager that automatically picks safe, cost-effective quant profiles per workload bucket while preserving latency SLOs. Design summary: 1) Budget Savant engine: observes recent per-tenant token/latency/cold-start patterns and computes a prefilling budget and prefetch TTL heuristics; 2) Quant Tier Manager: uses a lightweight regret-minimization loop that recommends quantization tier (fp16, int8, or hybrid 4-bit) per model/tenant cohort and ties recommendations to unit-econ delta thresholds; 3) Safe-fallbacks: runtime enforces a safe mode that uses a conservative profile when tail latency deviates beyond configured hysteresis. This PR implements the controller, telemetry hooks, CLI flags for dry-run, and a migration path for existing tenant defaults.

title: Variant orchestration protocol with adaptive holdpoints and explainable rewind
preview:
Motivation: Production rollouts increasingly require fine-grained checkpoints that pause promotion for human or automated validation while preserving deterministic replay and fast recovery. This PR introduces a Variant Orchestration Protocol (VOP) that adds adaptive holdpoints and an explainable rewind mechanism to the rollout runtime.

Summary: Implement VOP primitives, extend the rollout CRD with holdpoints/rewind_policy/priority_succession, add a snapshot-backed rewind store, and wire telemetry anchors into the decision loop.

Key goals: 1) allow staged canaries to pause automatically when a metric drift is detected, 2) enable deterministic rewind-to-checkpoint with an explainability trace for why a rewind was triggered, 3) support priority succession for traffic-lease handoff so higher-priority cohorts can reclaim traffic without full rollback.

Design highlights:
- Holdpoints: declarative objects attached to a rollout that define gating conditions (metric anchors, manual gates, smoke tests) and an adaptive timeout that grows/shrinks based on historical approval latency.
- Rewind store: lightweight snapshot format capturing request routing state, active KV cache pointers, and a compact trace of decisions (anchors evaluated and signal deltas). Snapshots are retained for the rewind window and garbage-collected by TTL.
- Explainable rewind: when the controller triggers a rewind, it emits an audit trace that links the snapshot id, the anchor conditions that failed, and a minimal repro traffic sample. The trace is surfaced in the Console with a summarized reason and a link to the snapshot replay job.
- Priority succession: when multiple cohorts request the same capacity, the scheduler honors an ordered succession list; if a higher-priority cohort needs traffic, lower-priority cohorts are gracefully drained to an earlier holdpoint instead of being fully rolled back.

Backwards compatibility: rollout CRD retains previous fields; new fields are optional. Existing rollout controllers will ignore VOP fields if not present.

Migration: a small adapter converts legacy pause/resume annotations into a single holdpoint when upgrading clusters.

Tests and validation:
- Unit tests for holdpoint evaluation logic (anchors + adaptive timeout heuristics)
- Integration tests: cluster-level canary that simulates p99 latency spike and verifies automatic holdpoint insertion + rewind emission
- E2E against canary harness: replay snapshot and validate decision trace reproduction
- Load test: ensure snapshot write/read does not add >2% latency to decision loop

Observability: new metrics (vop_holdpoint_evaluations_total, vop_rewinds_total, vop_snapshot_write_latency_seconds) and logs with structured fields for snapshot_id/anchor_set.

text:
Motivation: Production rollouts increasingly require fine-grained checkpoints that pause promotion for human or automated validation while preserving deterministic replay and fast recovery. This PR introduces a Variant Orchestration Protocol (VOP) that adds adaptive holdpoints and an explainable rewind mechanism to the rollout runtime.

Summary: Implement VOP primitives, extend the rollout CRD with holdpoints/rewind_policy/priority_succession, add a snapshot-backed rewind store, and wire telemetry anchors into the decision loop.

Key goals: 1) allow staged canaries to pause automatically when a metric drift is detected, 2) enable deterministic rewind-to-checkpoint with an explainability trace for why a rewind was triggered, 3) support priority succession for traffic-lease handoff so higher-priority cohorts can reclaim traffic without full rollback.

Design highlights:
- Holdpoints: declarative objects attached to a rollout that define gating conditions (metric anchors, manual gates, smoke tests) and an adaptive timeout that grows/shrinks based on historical approval latency.
- Rewind store: lightweight snapshot format capturing request routing state, active KV cache pointers, and a compact trace of decisions (anchors evaluated and signal deltas). Snapshots are retained for the rewind window and garbage-collected by TTL.
- Explainable rewind: when the controller triggers a rewind, it emits an audit trace that links the snapshot id, the anchor conditions that failed, and a minimal repro traffic sample. The trace is surfaced in the Console with a summarized reason and a link to the snapshot replay job.
- Priority succession: when multiple cohorts request the same capacity, the scheduler honors an ordered succession list; if a higher-priority cohort needs traffic, lower-priority cohorts are gracefully drained to an earlier holdpoint instead of being fully rolled back.

Backwards compatibility: rollout CRD retains previous fields; new fields are optional. Existing rollout controllers will ignore VOP fields if not present.

Migration: a small adapter converts legacy pause/resume annotations into a single holdpoint when upgrading clusters.

Tests and validation:
- Unit tests for holdpoint evaluation logic (anchors + adaptive timeout heuristics)
- Integration tests: cluster-level canary that simulates p99 latency spike and verifies automatic holdpoint insertion + rewind emission
- E2E against canary harness: replay snapshot and validate decision trace reproduction
- Load test: ensure snapshot write/read does not add >2% latency to decision loop

Observability: new metrics (vop_holdpoint_evaluations_total, vop_rewinds_total, vop_snapshot_write_latency_seconds) and logs with structured fields for snapshot_id/anchor_set.

Console changes: Rollout timeline gains checkboxes for holdpoints, a snapshot viewer with a replay button, and an explainable-rewind card.

Rollout plan:
- Rollout to staging feature-flagged for beta tenants
- Monitor vop_rewinds_total and snapshot GC metrics for 2 weeks
- Gradual enable across Dedicated tenants with opt-in in Console

title: Holdpoint-carousel phased promote with rehearsal-shadow replay and rollback playbook
preview:
Summary: Introduces a holdpoint-driven 'carousel' promote strategy aimed at safe staged promotes across session-sensitive workloads. The feature combines (1) holdpoint anchors — deterministic traffic cut points keyed by session cohorts, (2) a rehearsal shadow runner that replays representative traffic for each holdpoint prior to live promotion, and (3) a rollback playbook that encodes automatic and manual abort semantics with quarantine lanes.

Motivation: Multiple incidents where incremental canaries passed aggregate metrics but failed for sticky-session cohorts motivated a promotion strategy that ensures session-coherent rehearsals before traffic cutover. This PR implements the orchestration primitives and a default rollback playbook that operators can tune.

Design:
- Holdpoint controller: computes holdpoints from cohort selectors and session affinity metadata, maintains per-holdpoint state and progress checkpoints.
- Rehearsal shadow runner: lightweight traffic replayer that runs sampled requests against candidate model variants with KV-cache warmup and smoke checks.
- Rollback playbook: policy-as-data YAML-driven playbook mapping failure classes (latency spike, token-error regression, model drift) to actions (quarantine, immediate revert, partial drain, human-latch).
- Integration with Console: exposes per-holdpoint timelines, rehearsal badges, and playbook-executed actions.

Checklist:
- [x] Implement controller state machine and progress checkpoints
- [x] Add rehearsal runner with deterministic replay seed
- [x] Provide default rollback playbook and validation harness
- [x] Unit tests for controller transitions
- [x] Integration tests (simulated sessions)
- [x] Console telemetry and holdpoint UI hooks
- [x] Docs and quickstart snippet

Backwards compatibility: The new promote strategy is opt-in via rollout_profile: "holdpoint-carousel". Existing rollout profiles are unchanged. Config changes are additive and safe for clusters that do not opt-in.

Testing:
- Local unit test suites under test/unit or test/orchestrator run with new mock session registry.
- Integration harness has a new scenario that simulates 10k session handoffs across 3 holdpoints and validates session affinity + KV prewarm.
- Ran canary smoke on staging with 2 rehearsal runs; CI flake fixed by increasing test harness timeouts.

Rollout plan: Stage to staging for 48h, enable feature flag for 5% of traffic (non-critical customers), run two rehearsal cycles and collect per-holdpoint smoke metrics, then raise to 20% with automatic rollback monitoring on the playbook thresholds.

Related: builds on ENG-4321 (session-affine routing) and follows the telemetry contract in ENG-4399.

Files changed (high level):
- src/orchestrator/holdpoint_controller.py: new state machine and API hooks
- src/orchestrator/rehearsal_runner.go: sample-driven replay engine with KV warmup
- src/orchestrator/playbooks/rollback_playbook.yaml: policy-as-data examples
- console/ui/holdpoint_timeline.tsx: UI badges and timeline
- docs/rollouts/holdpoint-carousel.md: design + quickstart
- test/integration/holdpoint_carousel_test.go: integration scenario
- cfg/rollout_profiles/defaults.yaml: new profile entry

Operational concerns & mitigation:
- Cost: rehearsal runs increase token spend; playbook includes a rehearsal_budget parameter and sampling controls.
- Latency: KV warmup performed in rehearsal; live cutover waits for warmup checkpoint.
- Observability: added per-holdpoint smoke histograms and replay diffs; console links to sample traces.

Rollback semantics: the playbook exposes three abort modes: fast-revert (squash), partial-drain (graceful rollback of subsequent holdpoints), and quarantine (isolate traffic, escalate to human-latch). See rollback_playbook.yaml for mappings and default thresholds.

Documentation: A new page docs/rollouts/holdpoint-carousel.md includes conceptual diagrams, playbook examples, and a quickstart.

Migration notes: none required for existing profiles; operators must opt-in to use the new profile.

Open items:
- Add CLI tooling to generate rehearsal seeds from production trace (follow-up PR)
- Add deterministic trace replay for embeddings-heavy workloads (research task)

title: introduce-stream-sanitizer-window-and-functioncall-flush-to-preserve-json-span-contiguity
preview: Motivation: Intermittent production incidents and fuzz tests revealed cases where SSE chunk boundaries could split JSON object spans or function-call sentinel tokens in ways that make downstream parsers emit truncated or mis-ordered events. This caused structured-output consumers and OpenAI-compatibility wrappers to drop function-call arguments or mis-associate tokens when partial frames were replayed. This PR introduces a lightweight streaming sanitizer layer and a small reflow window that: 1) keeps a minimal lookback buffer for boundary repair, 2) normalizes delimiter emission order for function/tool-call sentinels, and 3) applies a safe escape-rewrite for split UTF-8/JSON token fragments. The description below summarizes changes, risks, testing, and rollout plan. Changes made: - runtime/sse/stream_window.go: new StreamSanitizer that maintains a sliding reflow window (configurable max bytes, default 4KB) and detects partial JSON spans using a fast heuristic (brace/quote counters + UTF-8 continuation checks). When a span crosses the window boundary the sanitizer defers emit and attempts repair by joining adjacent chunks or emitting a rescue-marker event. - runtime/sse/protocol.go: added deterministic ordering guarantees for sentinel emissions (heartbeat -> preamble -> function_call_start -> payload chunks -> function_call_end). This avoids reordered sentinel interleaving under high-concurrency flush. - openai/compat/adapter.py: adapter updated to understand the new rescue-marker event and to tolerate delayed function_call_end tokens; compatibility shim will buffer until a matching end or rescue marker. - runtime/kv/cache_adapter.rs: minor change to avoid flushing cached prefixes in the middle of an active function call stream. - tests/integration/sse_partial_json_test.go: new deterministic and randomized tests that simulate chunk-split UTF-8 and nested JSON objects across many micro-batches. - docs/compat/notes_sse_chunking.md: notes for integrators about rescue-marker semantics and upgraded adapter behavior. Implementation notes: - The sanitizer is intentionally conservative: by default it only defers emission for spans <4KB and recent activity; longer objects are passed through to avoid unbounded buffering. - UTF-8 sequences and escape sequences are validated with a fast-path routine; malformed runs trigger a rescue-marker plus the raw bytes (so higher-level telemetry can decide). - The functioncall sentinel ordering change is idempotent for well-formed streams; adapters that already respect function_call_start/end are unchanged. Rollout and migration: - Feature gated behind runtime flag "sse.stream_sanitizer.enabled" (default: off in canary). - Canary rollout to 5% of hosts for 48 hours; telemetry checks for increased latency, increased memory use, and rescued-frame rate. - If rescue rate >0.1% or p95 latency regression >10% we will roll back the gate. Testing and validation: - CI: added unit tests for boundary heuristics and integration test harness that replays recorded truncated-chunk incidents. - Staging: ran long-run fuzz and compatibility suite against openai-compat adapter; observed 0 parity regressions and eliminated a previously reproducible tool-call-argument-drop case. - Load test: negligible CPU increase on average, tail latencies increased by ~3ms in worst-case (within SLO). Review summary and discussion: - Jordan: "Core heuristic looks reasonable; ensure the window size is configurable per-customer since some private deployments may accept higher buffering." Ava: "Added config path and documented VPC override in installer notes." - Maya: "What about cascade effects with autoscaler when many hosts buffer?" Ava: "Sanitizer uses soft memory caps per-worker and will spill to disk after soft cap; we added a metric sse.sanitizer.spill_count and autoscaler alerts to the rollouts." - Ethan: "Adapter changes look safe but add a migration note for older clients." Ava: "Added compatibility shim to adapter.py that keeps old behavior if client declares legacy-mode." CI and merge: - CI initially caught a flaky test in the randomized harness; fixed by tightening the timing window and re-running. - All checks passed on the final run (unit, integration, lint, format, OSS compatibility). - Merge method: squash and merge. Related: ENG-4523 (streaming reliability), SUP-1290 (customer incident report). Release notes: "Fixes where SSE chunking could split JSON or function-call sentinels, causing truncated structured output for some OpenAI-compat consumers. Introduces a stream sanitizer and rescue-marker semantics; disabled by default during canary rollout." Risks and mitigations: "Increased memory under pathological inputs (mitigated via soft cap and spill-to-disk), and potential small p95 latency increase (observed <=3ms in load tests). Rollback plan included. Follow-ups: add server-side telemetry to track rescued-span types and a developer CLI to reassemble rescued frames for debugging.

title: streamline-crosssdk-auth-backoff-telemetry-and-stream-helper-polish
preview: Motivation: Customers and platform integrations reported inconsistent backoff configuration surface and sparse telemetry around auth/reauth flows across the three language SDKs. This made incident triage slower (missing 'auth.retry.count' and inconsistent backoff timings) and increased user friction when wiring streaming consumers. This PR harmonizes the public config surface, adds lightweight telemetry knobs, and provides small ergonomic helpers for streaming consumers in each SDK. Scope: 1) Normalize retry/backoff config to a single canonical schema (base_ms, max_ms, factor, jitter) and add parsing shims for legacy formats. 2) Emit normalized telemetry counters/timings for auth handshake, reauth events, and retry backoff windows (auth.retry.count, auth.retry.last_delay_ms, auth.heartbeat.missed). 3) Add an ergonomic StreamingConsumer helper (named StreamingAdapter in TypeScript/Go and streaming_helper in Python) that wires cancelation signals, backoff hints, and exposes an async iteration pattern compatible with existing examples. 4) Bump SDK versions: python->1.31.0, typescript->1.28.0, go->1.6.2. Checklist: - [x] Define canonical backoff schema and add parser in common/compat layer - [x] Implement telemetry emission (metrics + optional debug logging) - [x] Add streaming helper in each SDK with tests - [x] Update examples and README snippets - [x] E2E smoke tests against hosted API and replayed auth failure scenarios - [x] Version bumps and CHANGELOG entries Compatibility notes: - SDKs accept both the new canonical object and legacy tuple-based backoff configs (parser lives in sdk/common/backoff_parser). - Streaming helper is additive; existing streaming APIs remain unchanged. - Telemetry keys are additive and controlled by the SDK telemetry switch; customers who disable telemetry will not see the new metrics. Testing and validation: - Unit tests added for parser edge cases (zero/negative values clamp), telemetry tags (region, model), and streaming cancellation propagation. - Integration test harness replays a flaky auth endpoint to validate retry counts and heartbeat telemetry. - Manual smoke tested on Dedicated and Hosted endpoints for latency regressions; observed <1% p99 increase under synthetic load. Rollout plan: - Merge to main and cut patch SDK releases. - Publish Python, TS, Go packages and update quickstarts. - Monitor telemetry for first 72 hours for unexpected spikes in auth.retry.* metrics and listen for support tickets related to changed config shapes. Related: SUP-9821 (customer-reported missing auth telemetry), ENG-4217 (sdk parity initiative).

title: stabilize-sse-terminator-gate-and-ordered-handoff
preview:
Motivation: We continue to see production incidents where bursty backpressure and multibyte UTF-8 splits cause mid-json artifacts to be emitted to clients or function-tool call order to be inverted under heavy load. This PR introduces a conservatively scoped runtime change that (1) prevents emission of incomplete JSON spans by gating SSE 'data:' events until a terminator check passes, (2) normalizes trailing escape/runout sequences across adapters to avoid truncated UTF-8 and spurious delimiter insertion, and (3) sequences function/tool handoffs with a lightweight ordered-handoff token to preserve toolcall ordering during replay and reconnects.

Change summary: single continuous description of changes made and rationale: Implemented a "terminator gate" in the stream emitter that attaches a compact span anchor to outgoing chunk buffers; the gate delays flush of any buffer that does not contain a complete top-level Json object or a validated function-call sentinel. Introduced escape-runout normalizer that rewrites split UTF-8 sequences at chunk boundaries into safe continuation bytes and records the continuation length in the span anchor so downstream reassembly can detect and recover truncated codepoints. Added an ordered-handoff leash: a per-request sequence token (shallow, 64-bit monotonic id) carried in the SSE preamble and acknowledged by the acceptor, used only for delivery ordering of function/tool call events; this is intentionally separate from existing seq/checksum subsystems to reduce blast radius. Updated the OpenAI compatibility adapter to honor the new preamble and gate semantics so third-party clients see stable behavior with no mid-json emits.

Implementation details: - go/streaming/emitter.go: new TerminatorGate type, gate state machine and unit tests. - go/streaming/utf8_normalizer.go: runout detector and patch routine used before chunk flush. - openai_compat/sse_adapter.py: read preamble, validate span anchor, and hold partial frames until gate release. - js/sdk/streaming.ts: small client-side acceptor change to validate ack windows and continue reassembly on reconnect. - integrations/tests/sse_chunk_replay_test.go and js/tests/stream_tool_handshake_spec.ts: end-to-end cases for reconnects, UTF-8 split, and out-of-order toolcalls.

Tests & CI: Added a matrix of unit and integration tests that replicate the previously observed failure modes: forced chunk-splits in the middle of multibyte characters, SSE reconnect during buffered functioncall sequences, and simulated burst backpressure with random latency. Local runs reproduced the bug; CI initially failed one flaky integration (stream_tool_handshake_spec) which we hardened by adding a short jitter and explicit ack wait; final CI green.

Backward compatibility and rollout: Changes are feature gated behind stream.feature.terminator_gate (on by default for new deployments) and adapter-level opt-out for legacy clients. The ordered-handoff token is appended as a syntactic extension to the SSE preamble (preamble: seq=<N>;term=<anchor>) and will be ignored by older clients. We will monitor the streaming-err and fncall-order dashboards for regressions during the rollout; a fast rollback is available via feature flag toggle.

Risk and mitigation: The terminator gate can increase head latency at the boundary of large incomplete chunks; we mitigate by only gating when the emitter detects incomplete top-level JSON span and honoring a small soft-timeout (50ms) to prefer availability if the downstream is slow to respond. We also added a metrics bucket for gate-induced delay to track impact.

Related work: Complements ENG-1098 (sse-seq-resilience) and partially overlaps with ENG-1322 (fragment reassembly improvements). Incident SUP-887 contained traces used as test vectors.

Merge strategy: squash and merge after approvals and CI green. Post-merge: promote to staging with the feature flag enabled for 48-hour observability window.

Notes: This PR is intentionally scoped to the streaming surface and OpenAI adapter; it does not modify the persistent seq-checksum ledger. Future follow-ups: expand gate heuristics to support partial-JSON-with-metadata cases and add a lightweight telemetry label to correlate gate events with user traces.

Implementation details: - go/streaming/emitter.go: new TerminatorGate type, gate state machine and unit tests. - go/streaming/utf8_normalizer.go: runout detector and patch routine used before chunk flush. - openai_compat/sse_adapter.py: read preamble, validate span anchor, and hold partial frames until gate release. - js/sdk/streaming.ts: small client-side acceptor change to validate ack windows and continue reassembly on reconnect. - integrations/tests/sse_chunk_replay_test.go and js/tests/stream_tool_handshake_spec.ts: end-to-end cases for reconnects, UTF-8 split, and out-of-order toolcalls.

Tests & CI: Added a matrix of unit and integration tests that replicate the previously observed failure modes: forced chunk-splits in the middle of multibyte characters, SSE reconnect during buffered functioncall sequences, and simulated burst backpressure with random latency. Local runs reproduced the bug; CI initially failed one flaky integration (stream_tool_handshake_spec) which we hardened by adding a short jitter and explicit ack wait; final CI green.

Backward compatibility and rollout: Changes are feature gated behind stream.feature.terminator_gate (on by default for new deployments) and adapter-level opt-out for legacy clients. The ordered-handoff token is appended as a syntactic extension to the SSE preamble (preamble: seq=<N>;term=<anchor>) and will be ignored by older clients. We will monitor the streaming-err and fncall-order dashboards for regressions during the rollout; a fast rollback is available via feature flag toggle.

Risk and mitigation: The terminator gate can increase head latency at the boundary of large incomplete chunks; we mitigate by only gating when the emitter detects incomplete top-level JSON span and honoring a small soft-timeout (50ms) to prefer availability if the downstream is slow to respond. We also added a metrics bucket for gate-induced delay to track impact.

Related work: Complements ENG-1098 (sse-seq-resilience) and partially overlaps with ENG-1322 (fragment reassembly improvements). Incident SUP-887 contained traces used as test vectors.

Merge strategy: squash and merge after approvals and CI green. Post-merge: promote to staging with the feature flag enabled for 48-hour observability window.

Notes: This PR is intentionally scoped to the streaming surface and OpenAI adapter; it does not modify the persistent seq-checksum ledger. Future follow-ups: expand gate heuristics to support partial-JSON-with-metadata cases and add a lightweight telemetry label to correlate gate events with user traces.

title: OpenAI toolcall consensus resolver and affordance mapping
preview:
Context and motivation: We observed a class of customer bugs where OpenAI-compatible tool calls produced multiple candidate tool invocations or partially overlapping schema outputs in streaming scenarios (e.g., assistant suggests both 'create_ticket' and 'open_case' with overlapping fields). Existing heuristics (first-wins / highest-confidence) are brittle when tools have similar signatures or when streaming fragments arrive out-of-order. This PR implements a lightweight consensus resolver and an affordance mapping layer that: 1) aggregates partial and final tool outputs during a request, 2) scores candidates by schema affinity, declared affordances, and runtime provenance, and 3) emits a single canonical invoke envelope to downstream backends while preserving a decision provenance header for audit and debugging.

Summary of changes: Implemented consensus resolver in the OpenAI compatibility adapter, added affordance mapping proto/schema and config, extended streaming framer to tag tool fragments with sequence provenance, added unit/integration tests and a small docs page. Key goals: minimize latency impact for the common single-tool case, improve determinism for ambiguous tool outputs, and provide observability into the resolver decisions.

Commits (high-level): - Add consensus resolver component and public API + tests - Introduce affordance map proto and loader with migration guide - Extend streaming framer to include fragment provenance tags and repair logic - Emit telemetry (resolver.reason, resolver.score_vector, resolver.input_digest) - Add config flag and console toggle for rollout - Fix flakiness in integration test by adding deterministic seeds and timeouts

Files touched (high-level): - src/openai_compat/resolver.py: new consensus resolver, core scoring heuristics, tie-break rules - src/openai_compat/affordance.proto: new affordance schema, enums and mapping fields - runtime/streaming/framer.go: attach fragment_provenance headers and coalescing helpers - api/handlers/tool_invoke_handler.go: adapt to accept canonical envelopes + provenance header - docs/openai-compat/toolcall-consensus.md: design rationale and rollout instructions - tests/openai_compat/test_consensus.py: unit & integration coverage - telemetry/metrics.yaml: new metrics and labels (resolver_reason, resolver_winner, resolver_confidence) - examples/quickstarts/consensus-config.yaml: sample opt-in config

Design notes (condensed): - Scoring: we compute an affinity score combining schema overlap (tokenized field names & types), declared affordance weights (configured per-tool; default neutral), and runtime signals (model confidence, fragment completeness). Scores normalized to [0,1]. - Tie-breaking: prefer tools with explicit affordance dominance, then higher schema specificity (more required fields matched), then lower inferred risk (user-specified risk tags on affordance map). - Streaming safety: fragments carry a provenance vector {seq_id, is_final, partial_digest}. The framer coalesces fragments by seq_id. If two distinct tool bodies are both final, resolver attempts a lightweight merge by field union when schemas are compatible; otherwise it applies tie-break rules and emits the losing candidate as a telemetry artifact (not invoked). - Backwards compatibility: if consensusResolver=false or affordance map missing, behavior falls back to previous first-wins semantics. - Observability: each decision emits a resolver_provenance header with a compact JSON blob (string-encoded) and metrics are tracked for alerting on increased ambiguity rates.

Testing and CI: - Local unit tests added for scoring edge cases, schema affinity fuzz tests, and streaming fragment reordering. - Integration tests simulate two competing tools with overlapping outputs and assert canonical envelope invariants and telemetry emission. - CI initially failed on lint (line length and typo in proto comments); fixed and re-ran. Final CI: all checks passed, integration run (flaky tests) passed on second attempt.

Review conversation (highlights): - Diego Morales (reviewer): "Strong improvement for ambiguity cases. Two asks: (1) can we avoid copying full payloads into telemetry? keep digests. (2) add a small benchmark for common single-tool path to validate latency impact." Author response: implemented digest-only telemetry, added microbenchmark harness under perf/consensus and included results in the docs (latency ~+0.4ms p50). - Riley Patel: "Edge-case: merging fields when types differ (string vs object) — prefer conservative behavior and emit conflict reason." Author: adjusted merge to bail to tie-break and added a conflict_reason label. - Samir Gupta: requested clearer rollout plan and console toggle. Author: added config flag redwood.openai.consensusResolver and a console feature flag with canary to 10% by default.

Manual QA and rollout plan: - Feature gated behind config + console toggle. - Start with 10% canary in hosted, monitor metrics resolver_ambiguity_rate and resolver_invocation_failures for 48h. - If stable, increment to 50% and monitor SLOs for latency and error rates. - For Dedicated/Private customers, opt-in via config; documented migration steps in docs/openai-compat/toolcall-consensus.md.

Notes on future work: - Add a lightweight ML model to improve schema affinity for polysemantic tools (tracking as ENG-7740). - Expose per-customer affordance overrides via Console UI. - Consider a re-run/hedge strategy where losing candidate is invoked in background if confidence difference < threshold (opt-in).

Merge: squash and merge. Changelog entry included. Rollout toggle enabled off by default.

Related: SUP-4412 incident inspired tie-break completions; referenced earlier PRs for streaming framer improvements in runtime.

Contact: ava.chen@redwood.ai for follow-ups and rollout questions.

title: Add token-attribution prober and rollback-suggestion CI adapter
preview:
Motivation: We observed regressions that only manifest at the token level (e.g., a previously rare hallucinated sub-token pattern increasing frequency in certain prompt cohorts). Existing cohort-level SLOs catch change in aggregate metrics but not subtle token-attribution shifts. This PR introduces a lightweight token-attribution prober that runs as part of eval-harness smoke checks and a CI adapter that suggests minimally invasive rollback patches when attribution drift exceeds configured thresholds.

Summary: Adds a token-level attribution probe that integrates model attribution signals (attention-based saliency and logit-difference scoring) into the eval pipeline, produces per-prompt per-token drift heatmaps, and new CI logic that: 1) compares attribution fingerprints to historical anchors, 2) raises soft or hard failures depending on severity, and 3) emits rollback suggestions (prompt edits or anchor-replay patches) for triage. The description below details design, testing, and rollout.

What changed (high level):
- New module eval_harness/probers/token_attribution_prober.py: core probe implementation and pluggable attribution backends.
- New CI adapter eval_harness/ci/adapters/rollback_suggester_adapter.py: consumes probe output and converts detected regressions into actionable suggestions and GitHub-flavored comment payloads for PRs.
- Added a lightweight CLI hook bin/run-token-prober for local reproductions.
- Integrations: hooks registered in eval_harness/orchestrator/hooks.yaml and a new metrics exporter eval_harness/metrics/token_drift_metrics.py.
- Tests: unit and integration tests under tests/probers and tests/ci to validate detection logic and suggestion generation.
- Docs: docs/ci/token-attribution-prober.md with usage, thresholds, and recommended rollout knobs.

How it works (technical):
1) Attribution backends: the prober supports multiple attribution signals. By default we compute token saliency as delta in token logit probability when prefix tokens are ablated (fast approximation implemented) and augment with attention-weighted saliency when available from model traces. Backends are pluggable so teams can add model-specific explainers.
2) Fingerprint generation: for each prompt cohort we compute aggregated token-attribution histograms and normalize them into a fingerprint vector. We store epochal anchors (daily snapshots) and compute a symmetric KL + L2 composite distance to detect shifts.
3) CI gating: adapter compares current fingerprint to the pinned anchor for the target prompt set. Thresholds: soft (suggestion): distance > d_soft (defaults documented); hard (CI fail): distance > d_hard. Soft failures post a triage suggestion comment and attach a mini-report; hard failures block merge.
4) Rollback suggestions: when a soft or hard drift is detected we compute a minimal set of prompt edits or anchor-replay configurations (e.g., revert to prior canonical prompt variant or add an anchor replay entry) and include a suggested patch diff (applies to prompt-set YAMLs) that maintainers can accept with a single click. Suggestions are heuristic and flagged for human review.

Testing & validation:
- Unit tests for attribution math (100% branch coverage for the math kernels).
- Integration test that replays a seeded synthetic drift (see tests/probers/data/synthetic_drift.json) and asserts the adapter flags both soft and hard cases.
- End-to-end run included in CI that executes the prober in Docker with a small open model (weights mocked via deterministic scorer) to keep runtime <90s in smoke runs.

Performance & cost considerations:
- The prober has a fast mode (approximate logit delta with single-token ablations) used in CI smoke runs and a thorough mode (attention saliency + multi-token perturbations) used in nightly anchors. Default CI run uses fast mode to bound cost.

Backward compatibility and rollout plan:
- Default behavior is non-blocking (soft suggestions) for the first two weeks after merge; auto-blocking can be enabled via feature flag in orchestrator config.
- We ship a migration that backfills epochal anchors for the last 14 days; backfill job included at bins/backfill/token_anchor_backfill.py.

Operational signals and dashboards:
- Exposes Prometheus metrics: eval_token_drift_score_total, eval_token_drift_alerts{severity}, eval_token_probe_latency_seconds.
- Added Grafana dashboard JSON (dashboards/token-attribution.json) and an alert rule that triggers SRE paging only for sustained hard failures.

Checklist:
- [x] Implementation code
- [x] Unit & integration tests
- [x] Docs and usage guide
- [x] CI integration and smoke entry
- [x] Rollout plan and backfill job

Notes: This PR intentionally keeps the default thresholds conservative to avoid noisy CI failures during ramp. Teams with sensitive prompt-sets (security/compliance) can opt-in to strict blocking via the orchestrator config.

Related work: This complements the token-saliency smokecheck introduced previously and ties into the cohort-stability framework (ENG-4921) to add a token-granular signal for regression triage.

Manual reproduction: See docs/ci/token-attribution-prober.md for step-by-step; quick reproduction: bin/run-token-prober --cohort tests/probers/data/example_cohort.yaml --mode fast

Merge notes: squash-merged after an extra CI run to ensure the nightly backfill job locks anchors properly.

title: Adaptive kernel routing and KV-scheduler tweaks for Mistral/Qwen (GQA/MQA tuning)
preview: Motivation: production workloads showed inconsistent latency/throughput tradeoffs across model families (Mistral, Qwen, Llama variants) when using a single kernel selection and KV cache routing policy. This PR introduces an adaptive kernel routing layer and a lightweight KV-scheduler that chooses sharding and prefetch priorities per model and request workload, plus targeted GQA/MQA tuning knobs. The goals are: reduce p50/p95 latency variance, increase sustained throughput for mixed-sequence traffic, and avoid tail regressions when switching model variants. Summary of changes (single-line condensed): implement model profiles for kernel selection and quantization-aware algorithms; add KV-scheduler with per-route priority and prefetch window; continuous-batching changes to respect model affinity and early-flush heuristics; small Llama-specific attention correction for 8-bit MQA flows; Qwen memory layout workaround to avoid kernel thrash on long-context streams; add microbench harness + regression tests + docs updates. Checklist: 1) Perf harness and baseline numbers included 2) Unit tests for scheduler logic 3) Integration canary on perf-canary passed 4) Added release notes and console docs 5) Backwards-compatible config defaults. Commits summary: 1) "model-profiles: add adaptive kernel routing and profile schema" 2) "runtime: KV-scheduler implementation and tests" 3) "batcher: affinity and early-flush integration" 4) "mistral: 8-bit attention edge-case fix" 5) "qwen: continuous-kernel fallback for long context" 6) "docs: runtime tuning guide and release notes" Changed areas (high-level file list): runtime/kernel_router.cpp; runtime/kernel_selector.h; runtime/kv_scheduler.{cc,h}; runtime/batching/continuous_batcher.cpp; models/mistral/attention_fixes.cpp; models/qwen/kernel_fallback.cpp; perf/bench/kv_sched_harness.py; docs/runtime/optimizations.md; tests/runtime/test_kv_scheduler.py. Small illustrative snippets (kept minimal): added selection score calculation in kernel_router.cpp to prefer quantization-aware kernels for MQA flows; KV-scheduler uses LRU-backed prefetch window with a per-model weight. Tests and CI: initial CI run hit an intermittent GPU smoke failure in integration/gpu-smoke (nvidia-alloc-timeout), retried and passed; full perf-canary run showed 6-12% median latency reduction for Mistral-7B workloads (p50 down 8% median), and 18-24% increase in sustained tokens/sec for mixed short+long sequences on Qwen-14B when MQA profile enabled. Llama 2-13B 8-bit flows showed a 4% p95 improvement after the attention edge-case fix. Rollout plan: feature-flagged rollout for 2 weeks on dedicated cluster subset, followed by staged enablement per customer for private deployments; follow-up ENG task to instrument model-profile telemetry and auto-tune heuristics. Backwards compatibility: default kernel_router policy remains legacy until feature flag is enabled; config knobs documented. Release notes: performance: adaptive kernel routing and KV-scheduler reduce latency variance and improve throughput across Mistral/Qwen/LLM families; fixes: Mistral 8-bit attention correctness; notes: may improve or change token cost estimates due to different kernel selection. Related tickets: ENG-7684 (runtime perf initiative), ENG-7721 (KV scheduling design).

title: Introduce invocation segmentation + weighted-error suppressor and segment-level cost/latency metrics
preview:
Motivation: We frequently see noisy error spikes from downstream adapters and third-party integrations that cause alert storming and obscure true regressions. At the same time, token-level cost/latency rollups miss stagewise behavior inside long-running invocations (e.g., prefetch, model-run, post-process). This PR adds an invocation segmentation layer that (1) marks logical stages within an invocation, (2) emits segment-scoped metrics (latency, token-cost, errors), and (3) implements a lightweight weighted-error suppressor so transient noisy components don't overwhelm on-call channels.

Summary of changes: added a runtime segmenter used by the serving pipeline, new metrics (invocation_segment_latency_seconds_bucket, invocation_segment_token_cost_cents, invocation_segment_error_weight), tracing span naming for segments (invocation.segment.{name}), log enrichment with segment_id and segment_index, a Grafana dashboard (Invocation Segment Explorer), and a new SLO/alert policy that uses the weighted-error score and suppression windows. Feature gated under flag `obs.enable_invocation_segmentation` and disabled by default.

Commits:
- a1f3c9c - add core segmenter and simple API for stage boundaries (initial impl)
- b2d5e6a - wire metrics + otel span naming, dashboard JSON, unit tests for registry and histogram buckets
- c3f7aa1 - fix lint/staticcheck failures, add labels to histograms, tighten sampling in tests, update docs snippet

Files changed (high-level):
- src/serving/segmenter.rs - new: core segmenter, lifecycle hooks, in-memory segment context propagation
- src/telemetry/metrics_registry.yaml - new metric definitions and bucket configs
- src/tracing/trace_enricher.go - annotate spans with segment metadata and segment_id

title: Introduce presubmit cost sampler and toolchain stabilizer
preview: Context: CI noise from transitive dep updates and toolchain version flips has led to intermittent presubmit failures and unpredictable cost spikes in hosted runs. Motivation: reduce noise, gather sample-based cost telemetry, and stabilize the dev toolchain so presubmit failures are actionable rather than transient. What this PR does: 1) Adds a lightweight presubmit-cost-sampler pipeline (ci/presubmit/sampler.yaml) that runs on ~0.5% of PRs and records job-level time/cpu/mem/infra-cost estimates to the internal CI-telemetry store. 2) Introduces a toolchain-stabilizer service (infra/toolchain-stabilizer/) that: - pins a curated minimal set of tool versions for presubmit lanes, - warms language/OS-specific caches on ephemeral runners, - injects a cache-warm header into existing install steps. 3) Adds an opt-in "quiet-merge-window" gating policy (scripts/merge-window/README.md) that batches non-urgent merges during off-peak windows to reduce peak CI contention. 4) Adds a small metrics exporter (tools/ci-metrics/exporter.go) and a docs page (docs/ci/cost-sampler.md) describing cost metrics and how teams interpret them. Backwards compatibility: all changes are opt-in via two repo-level feature flags (repo settings): ci.cost_sampler and ci.toolchain_stabilizer. Default rollout: ci.cost_sampler=false, ci.toolchain_stabilizer=false. Rollout plan: stage 1 (internal): enable both flags for redwood/* small teams (1 week) and validate stability. Stage 2 (canary): enable ci.toolchain_stabilizer=true for 10% of active repos and ci.cost_sampler=true at 0.5% PR sample rate (2 weeks). Stage 3 (gradual): increase sampler to 2% and stabilizer to 50% if metrics are green. Testing done: - Unit tests for exporter and stabilizer boot sequence - End-to-end smoke against ephemeral runners (ci/presubmit/smoke) - Canary presubmit runs recorded in telemetry (see CI checks) Checklist: - [x] Feature flags implemented - [x] Minimal docs added - [x] Telemetry schema versioned (v1) - [x] Quick rollback path (feature flag off) Known limitations: sampler estimates are model-based and may over/under-estimate infra cost by ~10% initially; we will calibrate over next 2 weeks. Related: ENG-1456 (presubmit-costing), ENG-1489 (toolchain hygiene).

title: Introduce SSE anchor window and tool-call resume handshake to mitigate partial-JSON replay and delimiter drift
preview: Motivation: Streaming clients have sporadic partial-JSON replay and delimiter drift when network interruptions interleave with server-side tool-call handoffs. This causes downstream parsers to see duplicate delimiters or truncated JSON chunks that are hard to recover without application-level heuristics. This PR introduces two coordinated mechanisms: 1) an in-stream 'anchor window' that marks a recent stable byte offset snapshot for boundary resynchronization, and 2) a lightweight 'resume handshake' between server and client when resuming mid-stream (used only for OpenAI-compat streaming paths). Design summary: - Anchor window: server emits periodic anchor frames (sse:type:anchor) containing a short stable fingerprint and monotonic offset window. Clients use the last valid anchor to fast-resync when encountering partial JSON (instead of replaying from the start of the message or invoking expensive reassembly). - Resume handshake: when a client reconnects and provides the last-received-offset+fingerprint, the server validates and emits a concise resume-ack event with a tool-call lease marker if necessary; this lets in-flight tool/function call handoffs continue without duplicating sentinel delimiters. - Hedging & safety: If anchor validation fails, server falls back to existing seq-checkpoint reassembly; anchors are advisory only. No change to user-facing API surface for non-streaming endpoints. Implementation highlights: - service/sse/stream_reassembler.go: new AnchorWindow struct, anchor emitter, checksum-friendly fingerprint algorithm. - openai-compat/streaming_adapter.go: resume handshake integration with OpenAI-compatible SSE framing; retain backward compatibility when head_branch flag is unset. - sdk/typescript/streaming.ts: client-side resync path, anchor-aware parser, resume handshake helper. - tests/integration/streaming_resume_test.ts: end-to-end test exercising abrupt disconnect + resume with tool-call in progress. - docs/streams/anchor-window.md: protocol and client guidance. Rollout and compatibility: - Feature gate: default enabled on main for OpenAI-compatible streaming only. Non-compat clients ignore anchor frames (treat as comments). - Backward compatible: older clients continue to function; they simply ignore the anchor event. - Metrics: new counters (sse.anchor_emitted, sse.resume_success, sse.resume_fallback) added to Redwood Console. Testing and CI: - Added unit/integration tests. CI initially reported a flaky streaming-integration test (timeout under heavy load); re-ran the job after increasing test harness timeouts and added a deterministic reconnect schedule to eliminate nondeterminism. All checks green on final run. Security: Anchor fingerprints are non-secret, short (base64-encoded 8 bytes) and do not contain user payload. No changes to auth or token model. Risk and mitigation: - Risk: premature adoption by non-compat clients could create parser churn; mitigated by explicit feature gate and clear client guidance in docs. - Risk: slight CPU overhead for computing fingerprints; benchmarked and shows <0.5% added CPU at p95 under realistic workloads. Related work: builds on ENG-1129 (seq-checkpointing) and complements ENG-1184 (tool-call handoff stability). Checklist: - [x] Design doc reviewed by runtime team - [x] Unit + integration tests - [x] Backwards compat smoke tested - [x] Metrics and dashboard updates - [x] Docs added (anchor-window.md)

title: Heartbeat-driven circuit sanitizer and micro-shed orchestrator
preview:
Context: In recent regional incidents we observed long-lived 'open' circuit states in the regional circuit store after transient downstream errors. Those stale-open circuits caused unexpected tenant-level hard-denies during recovery windows and complicated regional failover. This PR introduces a heartbeat-driven reconciliation process (circuit-sanitizer) that periodically verifies circuit health and a lightweight micro-shedding orchestrator that applies tenant-scoped, time-bounded soft-throttles when SLO credit burn is detected. Motivation: reduce tenant-impact from stale circuits and provide a predictable, graceful degradation path that keeps higher-priority traffic flowing while soft-shedding noisy tenants.

What this changes (high level):
- New worker: control/circuit_sanitizer.go implements a heartbeat reconciler that scans regional circuit store, cross-checks active KV cache usage and recent error budgets, and transitions circuits from OPEN->HALF_OPEN when the target region reports healthy heartbeats.
- New policy engine module: control/micro_shed_orchestrator.go applies per-tenant micro-sheds (token-based soft throttles) with exponential decay and a configurable grace window. Micro-sheds only affect best-effort routes; SLO-backed routes are exempt by default.
- Config flags: runtime.feature.heartbeat_circuit_sanitizer (bool), runtime.policy.micro_shed_window_ms (int), runtime.policy.slo_exempt_routes (csv).
- Metrics & tracing: added metrics circuit_sanitizer_runs_total, circuit_sanitizer_fixes_total, micro_shed_applied_total, micro_shed_active_gauge{region,tenant_tier}, slo_credit_balance_gauge. Distributed trace spans added to reconcile loop to aid post-mortem.
- Tests: unit tests for reconciler logic and integration harness scenario that simulates a two-region outage and validates automatic reopen and micro-shed application.
- Rollout: guarded behind feature flag; staged rollout to non-production regions then to production with 10% region ramp, then 50%, then global. Feature includes an emergency kill switch and a read-only dry-run mode.

Design notes and tradeoffs: The reconciler intentionally prefers stale-circuit reopen over aggressive auto-close: we require both a healthy heartbeat and low recent error rate. Micro-shed decisions are intentionally conservative to avoid amplifying failures. The initial window defaults to 30s and uses a token decay to avoid long-term denial.

Testing & verification: ran local integration against perf-canary harness; added deterministic unit tests and a simulated fault injection scenario. Load tests show <2% additional median latency on control plane calls when reconciler is active. CI: flaky e2e initially failed due to timing race; increased retry/backoff and stabilized tests.

Migration and rollout plan: enable feature flag in staging for 48h; enable in us-east-1 at 10% traffic, monitor metrics (circuit_sanitizer_fixes_total, micro_shed_applied_total, error_budget_burn) and SLOs; proceed to 50% then full. If micro-shed_applied_total spikes unexpectedly, flip emergency-off flag and rollback.

Checklist: [x] unit tests [x] integration test scenario [x] metrics and traces [x] feature flag and emergency kill switch [x] rollout doc and runbook updated (runbooks/control-circuit-sanitizer.md)

title: adaptive-kernel-selector-and-prefetch-degradation-policy
preview:
Motivation: recent incidents showed increased p99 latency spikes when the runtime switched kernels aggressively for short sequences and simultaneously attempted speculative KV prefetching. This PR introduces an adaptive kernel selection policy plus a controlled prefetch degradation path that trades a small amount of throughput for deterministic correctness and lower tail latency under mixed workloads.

What this does (single-string description): Adds a KernelSelector that scores candidate kernels by estimated latency, numerical-safety score, and cache-friendliness; introduces a fallback budget token that bounds how often the fast path can be taken per model+device pair; implements a PrefetchDegradationPolicy that steps down speculative KV prefetch aggressiveness when p95/p99 latency or error-rate thresholds are exceeded; exposes a runtime flag --safe-mode to force conservative kernel and disable micro-prefetch heuristics for regulated workloads. The change coordinates with the continuous-batching layer to emit per-batch scoring metadata so the scheduler can avoid mixing high-risk fastpath batches with conservative ones.

Commits summary: compact, focused commits: 1) kernel selector core + scoring table (kernel_selector.rs, kernel_profiles.json), 2) fallback budget token implementation and metrics, 3) kv prefetch policy and prewarm adjustments (kv/prefetch.rs), 4) scheduler integration (scheduler/batch_manager.rs), 5) unit + microbench + e2e regression tests, 6) docs and changelog.

Files (high-level): runtime/kernel_selector.rs, runtime/kernel_profiles.json, scheduler/batch_manager.rs, kv/prefetch.rs, kv/cache_metrics.rs, serving/continuous_batching.rs, tests/perf/test_kernel_selector.py, tests/e2e/prefetch_degradation.yaml, docs/runtime/fallbacks.md, .github/workflows/perf.yml. Small illustrative snippets are included in the diff where helpful (see kernel selection scoring in runtime/kernel_selector.rs and the degradation thresholds in kv/prefetch.rs).

Performance notes: microbenchmarks on our internal perf-lab (A100 and T4 pools): adaptive selector reduces bad-kernel mispicks by 62% on mixed-length traces; p99 CPU-side tail reduced 18% on A100 with minimal p50 impact. Prefetch degradation cut memory thrash in KV-heavy workloads and removed a class of correctness-sensitive race where prefetch + rapid cache eviction led to stale KV lookups; we instrumented and validated by adding deterministic KV roundtrip tests.

Safety/correctness: numeric-safety score favours kernels with proven fp16/quantized stability for the model; the fallback budget ensures we do not silently oscillate between kernels (avoids thrash). The --safe-mode flag forces: 1) kernel policy to 'conservative', 2) prefetch disabled, 3) strict KV validation on every cache hit (adds latency but improves determinism). Documentation in docs/runtime/fallbacks.md explains when to use safe-mode (regulated/enterprise customers, incident mitigation).

Testing: added unit tests for kernel scoring edge cases, microbench harness to simulate mixed sequence-length streams, and an end-to-end scenario that reproduced the original incident locally. CI matrix covers x86 A100/T4 runners; a flaky perf test (tests/perf/test_batching_latency.py) initially failed and was rerun once. All checks passed on final run: build, lint, unit, microbench, and e2e regression.

Review thread (condensed into single string):
- Ava Chen (review): \"Can we add a telemetric signal for fallback-budget depletion? Want to ensure operators see throttling events.\"
- Leah (author): \"Added metrics: runtime.fallback_budget.used and runtime.fallback_budget.reset_count; documented in metrics.md.\"
- Miguel Torres (review): \"Concerned about memory overhead of prefetch accounting on large-model KV, suggest bounded history window.\"
- Leah: \"Implemented ring-buffer accounting with configurable window; default 128 entries, configurable via env VAR.\"
- Priya Nair (review): \"Please justify safe-mode defaults and add opt-in threshold tuning.\"
- Leah: \"Added configuration docs and conservative defaults that mirror existing SLA targets; included a small utility to translate latency SLOs to prefetch thresholds.\"
- Final: Approvals from Ava and Priya after addressing comments; Miguel requested a follow-up for telemetry retention, tracked in ENG-4210.

CI summary: build ✓, lint ✓, unit tests ✓, microbench ✓, e2e regression ✓ (one flaky perf test rerun). Post-merge: perf dashboards updated automatically via perf-lab webhook to include runtime.fallback_budget.* metrics.

Release notes: \"runtime: add adaptive kernel selection and KV prefetch degradation policy to reduce tail latency and provide a --safe-mode for deterministic deployments. See docs/runtime/fallbacks.md for configuration and operational guidance.\"

Merge: Squash-merged to main after approvals and CI green. Follow-ups: ENG-4210 (telemetry retention), ENG-4299 (experiment: dynamic fallback budget tuning via RL controller).
