This PR introduces an adaptive kernel selection policy plus a controlled prefetch degradation path that trades a small amount of throughput for deterministic correctness and lower tail latency under mixed workloads.

What this does (single-string description): Adds a KernelSelector that scores candidate kernels by estimated latency, numerical-safety score, and cache-friendliness; introduces a fallback budget token that bounds how often the fast path can be taken per model+device pair; implements a PrefetchDegradationPolicy that steps down speculative KV prefetch aggressiveness when p95/p99 latency or error-rate thresholds are exceeded; exposes a runtime flag --safe-mode to force conservative kernel and disable micro-prefetch heuristics for regulated workloads. The change coordinates with the continuous-batching layer to emit per-batch scoring metadata so the scheduler can avoid mixing high-risk fastpath batches with conservative ones.

Commits summary: compact, focused commits: 1) kernel selector core + scoring table (kernel_selector.rs, kernel_profiles.json), 2) fallback budget token implementation and metrics, 3) kv prefetch policy and prewarm adjustments (kv/prefetch.rs), 4) scheduler integration (scheduler/batch_manager.rs), 5) unit + microbench + e2e regression tests, 6) docs and changelog.

Files (high-level): runtime/kernel_selector.rs, runtime/kernel_profiles.json, scheduler/batch_manager.rs, kv/prefetch.rs, kv/cache_metrics.rs, serving/continuous_batching.rs, tests/perf/test_kernel_selector.py, tests/e2e/prefetch_degradation.yaml, docs/runtime/fallbacks.md, .github/workflows/perf.yml. Small illustrative snippets are included in the diff where helpful (see kernel selection scoring in runtime/kernel_selector.rs and the degradation thresholds in kv/prefetch.rs).

Performance notes: microbenchmarks on our internal perf-lab (A100 and T4 pools): adaptive selector reduces bad-kernel mispicks by 62% on mixed-length traces; p99 CPU-side tail reduced 18% on A100 with minimal p50 impact. Prefetch degradation cut memory thrash in KV-heavy workloads and removed a class of correctness-sensitive race where prefetch + rapid cache eviction led to stale KV lookups; we instrumented and validated by adding deterministic KV roundtrip tests.

Safety/correctness: numeric-safety score favours kernels with proven fp16/quantized stability for the model; the fallback budget ensures we do not silently oscillate between kernels (avoids thrash). The --safe-mode flag forces: 1) kernel policy to 'conservative', 2) prefetch disabled, 3) strict KV validation on every cache hit (adds latency but improves determinism). Documentation in docs/runtime/fallbacks.md explains when to use safe-mode (regulated/enterprise customers, incident mitigation).

Testing: added unit tests for kernel scoring edge cases, microbench harness to simulate mixed sequence-length streams, and an end-to-end scenario that reproduced the original incident locally. CI matrix covers x86 A100/T4 runners; a flaky perf test (tests/perf/test_batching_latency.py) initially failed and was rerun once. All checks passed on final run: build, lint, unit, microbench, and e2e regression.

Review thread (condensed into single string):
- Ava Chen (review): \"Can we add a telemetric signal for fallback-budget depletion? Want to ensure operators see throttling events.\"
- Leah (author): \"Added metrics: runtime.fallback_budget.used and runtime.fallback_budget.reset_count; documented in metrics.md.\"
- Miguel Torres (review): \"Concerned about memory overhead of prefetch accounting on large-model KV, suggest bounded history window.\"
- Leah: \"Implemented ring-buffer accounting with configurable window; default 128 entries, configurable via env VAR.\"
- Priya Nair (review): \"Please justify safe-mode defaults and add opt-in threshold tuning.\"
- Leah: \"Added configuration docs and conservative defaults that mirror existing SLA targets; included a small utility to translate latency SLOs to prefetch thresholds.\"
- Final: Approvals from Ava and Priya after addressing comments; Miguel requested a follow-up for telemetry retention, tracked in ENG-4210.

CI summary: build ✓, lint ✓, unit tests ✓, microbench ✓, e2e regression ✓ (one flaky perf test rerun). Post-merge: perf dashboards updated automatically via perf-lab webhook to include runtime.fallback_budget.* metrics.

Release notes: \"runtime: add adaptive kernel selection and KV prefetch degradation policy to reduce tail latency and provide a --safe-mode for deterministic deployments. See docs/runtime/fallbacks.md for configuration and operational guidance.\"

Merge: Squash-merged to main after approvals and CI green. Follow-ups: ENG-4210 (telemetry retention), ENG-4299 (experiment: dynamic fallback budget tuning via RL controller).

title: Add ensemble-score-aggregator and CI regression hook for composite eval metrics
preview: Motivation: teams running multi-metric evals need a single, auditable signal for CI gating that reflects weighted regressions across prompt sets and metric families. This PR introduces an "ensemble score" aggregator that computes a weighted composite from a configurable set of metrics (e.g. BLEU, ROUGE-L, exact-match, embedding-sim) and a CI hook that fails builds when the ensemble score or any high-priority submetric regresses past configured thresholds. What changed: added aggregator implementation, config schema, CI integration, unit + integration tests, docs and migration notes. title: Ephemeral playbook preview + safe-autoreply gateway for low-severity alerts
preview:
Motivation: On-call responders spend time drafting and validating small mitigation steps for noisy, low-severity alerts (timeouts, retries, downstream 4xx spikes). This PR introduces two related features to reduce cognitive load and MTTD: (1) an ephemeral playbook preview UI/CLI that generates a short, editable sequence of suggested remediation steps from the incident context and recent telemetry; (2) a Safe Autoreply Gateway that can optionally send gated automated replies to ticketing/chat channels for low-risk alerts (acknowledgement + recommended self-serve steps), with rate-limiting, audit trails, and kill-switches.

What it does: - Ephemeral playbook preview: incidentctl preview-playbook <alert-id> produces a compact, human-readable playbook draft (3-5 steps) derived from the runbook templates, recent traces, and cached diagnostics. Previews are ephemeral (TTL 24h) and stored encrypted in the incident-bot ephemeral store. Previews can be edited in-place from the Console or approved via CLI. - Safe Autoreply Gateway: a configurable policy engine that decides whether to auto-respond to incoming alerts. Policies are evaluated against signal heuristics (severity, noisiness, alert fingerprint frequency, recent self-serve success) and a safety checklist (no creds/ops writes, no scaling actions). Autoreplies are templated, include a short playbook excerpt and links to troubleshooting docs, and are logged with signature metadata. The gateway supports dry-run and sample modes before going live.

Design constraints & safety: - Opt-in per-service and globally disabled by default. - Rate-limits per-channel and per-alert-fingerprint. - Approval workflows: autoreplies can be auto-approved, require single reviewer sign-off, or only operate in dry-run depending on policy tier. - Revocation & audit: every autoreply has a revoke endpoint and is recorded in audit logs (KMS-backed). - Privacy: telemetry snippets included in previews are sanitized via existing redaction rules; we added an extra redaction pass for token-like strings.

Checklist: - [x] Implementation for preview generation service and CLI command - [x] Safe Autoreply policy evaluator and queue - [x] Audit/logging hooks + KMS envelope for ephemeral previews - [x] Tests: unit tests for policy rules, e2e test that runs dry-run scenario - [x] Docs: console docs + quickstart for Ops to opt in - [x] Feature flag gating and rollout plan - [x] Add metrics: autoreply.count, preview.generated, preview.accepted, preview.rejected, autoreply.revoked

Testing and rollout: We exercised the feature in a dedicated staging workspace for two weeks. Test scenarios included: noisy webhook storms, ticketing loops, and low-severity dependency failures. CI initially failed due to a flaky snapshot test (tests/test_playbook_preview_snapshot.py) which was quarantined and retried; subsequent runs passed. Rollout plan: enable in sample team via feature flag for 48h in canary; collect preview.accepted rate and autoreply.revoked as safety signals; gradual expansion to more teams if safe.

Operational notes: - CLI: incidentctl preview-playbook --alert ALRT-.... --dry-run - Console: Preview shows a diffable panel and an 'Accept & Send' button. - Safety defaults: autoreply throttled to 1 per 30m per-channel and 5 per hour per-fingerprint. - Revert: feature_flag incident.ephemeral_playbook = false disables preview generation and auto-enqueueing for autoreplies immediately.

Related work: linear tickets ENG-4231 (playbook preview generator), ENG-4302 (autoreply policy engine). This PR is intentionally focused on orchestration and safety primitives; follow-ups will add LLM-based suggestion tuning and language localization.

title: Entropy-regulated floodgate with failsafe scorer and staged cutover
preview:
Motivation: We see intermittent quality regressions after model promotes that are not captured by traditional SLO signals (latency/error). These regressions correlate with increases in output unpredictability for certain prompt slices (high diversity/entropy) which can indicate semantic drift or hallucination spikes. This PR introduces an entropy-regulated floodgate and a composite failsafe scorer to make staged rollouts safer and more automatic.

What this change does: Adds an "entropy" metric (normalized token-level surprisal/diversity) to the rollout decision pipeline, implements a floodgate controller that can throttle promotion traffic when entropy or combined risk exceeds safe thresholds, and a failsafe scorer that synthesizes latency, error-rate, and entropy into a single risk score. The controller supports simulation mode, manual override, and a calibration endpoint. Also includes a small e2e simulation harness to exercise staged promotions against synthetic traffic.

Key behaviors: 1) During shadow runs we compute per-slice entropy and surface it to telemetry with a 99/95/50 quantile breakdown. 2) The floodgate enforces a soft throttle when composite score > warn_threshold and a hard hold when > abort_threshold. 3) A short rolling window is used so transient spikes do not immediately abort a promote; the scorer exposes a decay parameter. 4) Operators can run a dry-run simulator which replays historical shadow traffic and produces a predicted pass/fail timeline.

Backwards compatibility: All runtime defaults are off. Existing rollout policies unchanged until teams opt into the new scorer or enable the floodgate via rollout policy flags.

Checklist: - [x] Entropy metric and telemetry export - [x] Floodgate controller and config flags - [x] Failsafe scorer library and unit tests - [x] Simulation harness and e2e smoke - [x] Docs and runbook draft - [x] Added calibration API and feature flag gating

Notes on safety: We designed thresholds conservatively and included a manual override path (operator approval button) with audit logging. There is a forced cooldown window to prevent flip-flopping when entropy hovers near the threshold.

Related: ENG-3421 (model-lifecycle regressions), ENG-3509 (telemetry enrichment for quality signals).

title: Cost Harbor: policy adapter and mini-batcher defaults for tenant-friendly unit-econ
preview: Context: We observed recurring tenant-level unit-econ regressions when autoscaling and batch-routing heuristics chose overly large batch shapes or aggressive quantization tiers for low-SLA tenants. This PR introduces 'Cost Harbor' — a lightweight policy adapter that evaluates tenant budget/latency classes at the gateway and applies conservative mini-batcher defaults and quant-tier caps before requests reach the scheduler. Motivation: reduce tail regressions and unexpected unit-cost spikes for cost-sensitive tenants without changing global batcher behavior. Scope: 1) gateway policy adapter service (inline library + small HTTP hook) 2) new tenant-default presets: mini-batcher shapes, max-quant-tier, safe-prefill window 3) CLI preview tool (redwood cost-harbor preview) to dry-run policy decisions against tenant sample traces 4) telemetry hooks and unit-econ regression alerts. Checklist: - [x] design doc and simulation (link: Linear ENG-7812) - [x] implementation of policy adapter and integration tests - [x] CLI preview with dry-run mode and CSV output - [x] e2e smoke test against local scheduler - [x] observability dashboard (cost-harbor tenant view) Summary of commits: - feat(policy): add cost-harbor adapter and config schema - feat(cli): add redwood cost-harbor preview command with sample replay - feat(runtime): apply mini-batcher hints to scheduler admission - fix(test): deterministic sample harness for preview tests - infra: add cost-harbor dashboard panels and alert rules High level files touched: - gateway/policies/cost_harbor.go (new) - cli/cost_harbor_preview/main.go (new) - scheduler/mini_batcher_hints.go (mod) - configs/defaults/cost_harbor_presets.yaml (new) - telemetry/dashboards/cost_harbor.json (new) - integrations/testdata/tenant-sample-small.json (new) Testing and CI: Unit tests cover policy evaluation matrix (budget x latency class x workload shape). Integration tests run a 50-tenant micro-sim and assert mean unit-cost reduction for budgeted tenants or no more than 5% latency regression for high-SLA tenants. CI flake: an early run failed due to a race in the preview harness — fixed by adding deterministic seeding. Runtime performance: applying the adapter adds ~120us median overhead at gateway (measured in local perf run) but reduces high-cost tail events by 18% in the synthetic tenant mix. Rollout plan: - Canary on dedicated staging region for two days with tenants flagged via feature-flag - Monitor unit-econ dashboard and alert thresholds for regressions - Gradual 25% -> 50% -> 100% rollouts pending no alerts Observability/telemetry: emits policy.apply, policy.decision, policy.fallback metrics and a per-tenant unit-cost delta metric emitted when adapter is active. Added Grafana panels to show tenant distribution and sample dry-run comparisons. Backwards compatibility and fallback: adapter uses config-driven safe-fallbacks (no-op) if scheduler rejects hints; it will never increase batch size or request higher quant tiers than requested. Security notes: adapter config stored in tenant config store with RBAC: only infra/admin roles can modify presets. Known limitations and follow-ups: - Prefill heuristics currently conservative for multi-model requests; follow-up to incorporate model-level cost signatures (ENG-7890) - CLI preview needs a richer sampling UI (future PR) Release notes: - Introduces Cost Harbor gateway policy adapter which applies conservative mini-batcher and quant-tier defaults for budgeted tenants - Adds redwood cost-harbor preview CLI for dry-run verification - Adds telemetry and dashboards to monitor unit-econ impact Impact: Expected to reduce unexpected cost spikes for budgeted tenants and provide operators a lower-risk knob for tenant defaults. Merge notes: squash-merged into main. Links: Linear ENG-7812 (design), Linear ENG-7890 (follow-up), SUP-4432 (support ticket that motivated change).

title: introduce-observability-notebook-and-token-cost-annotator
preview: Motivation: operators need a single, queryable snapshot that ties traces, metrics and token-level cost for a given invocation so oncall and SRE can do fast RCA without stitching multiple services. This PR introduces the Observability Notebook: a minimal server-side snapshot format and a lightweight UI template that surfaces token_cost slices, per-span enrichments and a prompt-shape baseline. The Notebook is backed by a small "cost baseline store" that keeps per-model/prompt-shape median per-token cost for drift detection. What this PR does: 1) adds an invocation snapshot producer (src/observability/notebook.py) that bundles trace spans, token-cost histogram, KV-cache warmup events, and a compact provenance map; 2) enriches spans with token_bucket_label and token_phase tags (pkg/tracing/enrichers/token_annotator.go); 3) emits token_cost.slice metrics (per-invocation slice) and token_cost.fingerprint from the producer to telemetry (telemetry/metrics_registry.yaml, pubsub/transformers_cost.py); 4) a console dashboard template for quick inspection and drilldown (dashboards/observability-notebook.json) and docs for oncall interactions (docs/observability/notebook.md); 5) feature flag and rollout policy (infra/fflags.yaml) so we can canary the notebook in Dedicated and Hosted; 6) unit tests (tests/test_notebook.py) and small e2e harness adjustments. Backwards compatibility: snapshot producer is behind a flag and uses existing trace/span formats with additive span tags so there are no breaking changes to downstream consumers. Rollout plan: enable 5% of traffic for internal orgs and Dedicated test tenants for 48 hours, collect adoption and retention metrics, then broaden. Link to design doc and demo: Linear ENG-4521 (includes mockups) and a short demo recording attached in comment. Checklist: - [x] library + producer - [x] span annotator - [x] metrics registration - [x] dashboard template - [x] docs and runbook snippet - [x] tests - [x] feature flag and rollback path Observability gains: faster mean-time-to-insight for token-cost anomalies, one-click RCA for long-tail high-cost invocations, and a baseline-driven alert that reduces noisy token-drift pagering by suppressing alerts under baseline-aligned drift. Caveats and limitations: snapshot increases per-invocation metadata footprint; notebook payloads are compressed and retention defaults to 7 days behind the flag. We will iterate on retention and indexing strategies after canary results.

title: Autotuned throttle + prefetch coordinator to reduce streaming first-byte latency
preview: Motivation: streaming requests under mixed workloads exhibited spiky first-byte latency (TTFB) and poor tail behavior when KV prefetches and microbatch drains collided with kernel selection. This PR introduces an autotuned coordinator that couples per-stream throttle windows with probabilistic KV prefetch warming and drain prioritization, aiming to reduce median and tail TTFB without regressing throughput. Key ideas: 1) Per-stream latency budgets derived from SLA config and recent percentiles drive a soft throttle that caps emission window size and dynamically stretches inter-token yield points. 2) A lightweight prefetch score uses recent KV cache hit/miss ratios, token generation velocity, and kernel warm indicators to trigger prefetches earlier for likely-follow prefixes. 3) Cooperative microbatch drain ordering: streams with tighter budgets and active prefetches are prioritized for kernel allocation and KV reheating. 4) Autotuner observes background load signals (device utilization, kernel queue depth, histogram of token latencies) and adjusts throttle aggressiveness and prefetch lead-time using exponential moving averages. This was implemented with low-overhead data structures and fast-path checks to avoid impacting the hot token loop. The description below outlines changes, benchmarks, tests and rollout plan.
text: Motivation: streaming requests under mixed workloads exhibited spiky first-byte latency (TTFB) and poor tail behavior when KV prefetches and microbatch drains collided with kernel selection. This PR introduces an autotuned coordinator that couples per-stream throttle windows with probabilistic KV prefetch warming and drain prioritization, aiming to reduce median and tail TTFB without regressing throughput. Key ideas: 1) Per-stream latency budgets derived from SLA config and recent percentiles drive a soft throttle that caps emission window size and dynamically stretches inter-token yield points. 2) A lightweight prefetch score uses recent KV cache hit/miss ratios, token generation velocity, and kernel warm indicators to trigger prefetches earlier for likely-follow prefixes. 3) Cooperative microbatch drain ordering: streams with tighter budgets and active prefetches are prioritized for kernel allocation and KV reheating. 4) Autotuner observes background load signals (device utilization, kernel queue depth, histogram of token latencies) and adjusts throttle aggressiveness and prefetch lead-time using exponential moving averages. This was implemented with low-overhead data structures and fast-path checks to avoid impacting the hot token loop. The description below outlines changes, benchmarks, tests and rollout plan.

title: Add predictive concurrency allocator with SLO-gating for canary routing
preview: Motivation: Canary evaluations often surface regressions caused by unexpected concurrency pressure on GPUs and model KV caches. This PR introduces a predictive concurrency allocator that uses short-window time-series features and a lightweight online estimator to predict occupancy and limit per-model concurrency before tail latency exceeds SLO thresholds. It ties into the existing canary pipeline so canaries can gate rollouts automatically when predicted saturation is likely. Design summary: - Runtime component: a new allocator service that subscribes to per-model throughput/latency streams and predicts short-term concurrency headroom using exponential smoothing and a small linear regressor. The allocator returns a recommended concurrency cap and a confidence score. - Canary integration: canary scheduler consults the allocator before opening traffic; if predicted tail-latency exceeds configured SLO margin the canary is paused and a soft rollback signal emitted. - Fallbacks: when the allocator is unavailable, we fall back to the static concurrency profiles. - Observability: new metrics (allocator_predicted_headroom, allocator_confidence, allocator_trigger_reason) and SLI dashboards added. - Config: exposed as a tunable in perf-canary Helm values with per-tenant overrides and a dry-run mode. Testing and validation: - Unit tests for estimator logic and boundary cases. - Integration tests in the perf-canary harness that replay historical traces and validate that the allocator reduces tail latency violations by ~35% in our samples. - Canary simulation suite to stress different traffic shapes (bursts, long tails, cold-start). Rollout plan: - Start in dry-run for 48h on canaries only (no gating). - Enable SLO gating for canaries with conservative thresholds for 2 weeks. - Gradually enable for low-risk models before wide rollout. Related work: builds on ENG-2134 (previous canary instrumentation) and complements the existing adaptive probe scheduler. Files changed (high level): list below details the main touched modules and rationale.

title: hackathon-2025-inference-ui-demo
preview: Motivation: lightweight hackathon project to build a minimal interactive demo that visualizes inference internals (batching, KV cache hits/misses, token throughput) and exposes a live-coded frontend that can replay sample requests. This PR introduces a small Next.js front-end, a mock serverless backend that simulates Redwood's batching behavior for demo purposes, and simple instrumentation endpoints that surface synthetic metrics. The goal is a portable demo that teams can run locally or in a short demo environment to explain inference tradeoffs. Changesets: added frontend pages and components under /demo-ui, a tiny express-compatible demo server under /demo-server, a scripts/serve-demo.sh helper, and a README with run instructions and screenshots. Checklist: - [x] demo UI with token stream playback - [x] mock batching simulator with configurable batch size/latency - [x] instrumentation endpoints (json) for KV cache stats - [x] runbook in README for local demo and deploy to Vercel - [x] basic accessibility/keyboard nav on UI Notes on fidelity: this intentionally uses a mocked runtime (no real model weights) to keep the demo lightweight; it simulates latency, batching, and KV cache signals deterministically. This is a hackathon artifact intended for internal demos, teaching, and outreach; not production code. Related work: references a few Linear tickets for follow-ups and a separate redwood/examples repo demo (links in README). Review summary: small iterative review cycle focused on UX clarity, test flakiness, and CI portability. Merging notes: squash merge, tidy up demo server env variables in follow-up PR.
